commit.message:
Add NASA FIRMS, OpenAQ, object detection, lighting analysis, fix ADR statuses

Extended sensor collectors:
- NASA FIRMS: near-real-time fire detection within 50km
- OpenAQ: air quality (PM2.5, NO2, O3) from nearest stations
- Object detection: luminance-region analysis (screens, colored objects)
- Lighting history: luminance + color temperature (warm/cool/neutral)
- collect_extended(): runs all collectors including new ones

ADR fixes: updated 7 stale statuses (Proposed→Accepted, Partially→Implemented).
RuView ADR-044 conflict resolved (provisioning tool → ADR-050).

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

Add ruos-sensors: opt-in consent + 30 data source collector (Rust)

Ed25519-signed consent system (ADR-SYS-0023):
- Default ON: system-metrics, network-stats, csi-sensing, geo-satellite
- Opt-in required: camera, audio, bluetooth, radar, household-patterns
- SHA-256 signed consent records at ~/.config/ruos/consent.json
- CLI: grant, revoke, status, list, collect, daemon

Collectors (ADR-SYS-0024):
- Thermal: CPU/GPU temperature from /sys/class/thermal/
- GPU: nvidia-smi utilization, power, temp, memory
- Power: RAPL energy counters
- Disk: usage from df
- Display: lock/unlock state
- USB: device count
- WiFi: network count, signal quality
- Network: RX/TX traffic volume
- Household: CSI motion-based quiet/active detection
- Audio: ambient RMS noise level (no recording)
- Bluetooth: anonymous device count
- External: UV index, sunrise/sunset from Open Meteo

Validated: 7 sources collected, consent working, brain storage confirmed.
8.4 MB Rust binary.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

docs(readme): plainer language, clearer introduction

Rewrites the landing copy for humans who don't already know what
"DiskANN", "MCP", or "AIDefence" mean. Leads with what the plugin
does FOR the user in three plain-English paragraphs, then walks
through a five-minute setup before any jargon lands.

Structure
---------
  - Hero + three badges
  - "What this plugin does, in plain English" — three concrete user
    outcomes, no acronyms
  - Five-minute setup with copy-paste commands
  - "What you can do with it" — verb-first table
  - Architecture diagram captioned as "How it fits together"
  - MCP config snippets for Claude Code / Codex / Gemini
  - Safety & privacy as full sentences, not bullets
  - Troubleshooting section for the four most common pitfalls
  - Comparison table vs obsidian-mind and Smart Connections
  - Honest roadmap link + issues CTA
  - Credits acknowledging RuVector, pi.ruv.io, obsidian-mind

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

initial: obsidian-brain plugin — RuVector Brain bridge for Obsidian

Ships a dedicated distribution of the plugin developed at
ruvnet/RuVector under examples/obsidian-brain/. Installable via BRAT
against ruvnet/obsidian-brain.

Capabilities
------------
  - Semantic search (Cmd+Shift+B) over DiskANN, 384-dim
    bge-small-en-v1.5 via ruvultra-embedder (candle-cuda)
  - Q&A modal (Cmd+Shift+K) — retrieval-grounded across local + pi
  - Related side-panel with keyboard nav
  - Auto-index on save (AIDefence-scanned, hash-deduped) with offline
    queue that retries every 30s
  - Bulk-sync vault → brain with progress + filters
  - Graph overlay: tag:#brain/<category> color groups
  - DPO preference pairs (mark chosen / pair / export)
  - pi.ruv.io: pull, search, publish (write-through), status
  - Brain Ops dashboard: workload, training stats, WAL checkpoint,
    DPO JSONL export
  - Daily recall (memories from this day in prior years)
  - Agent access (MCP) — cross-linked to mcp-brain-server

Tests
-----
  - 9 protocol tests against real mcp-brain-server-local subprocess
  - 6 pi.ruv.io protocol tests (gated on BRAIN_API_KEY) incl. POST
    /v1/memories write-through
  - 11 real-Obsidian harness checks under xvfb-run

CI
--
  - .github/workflows/test.yml — typecheck + build on every PR
  - .github/workflows/release.yml — build and publish main.js +
    manifest.json + styles.css on tag push (v*)

Implements ADR-152 / ADR-SYS-0025 from ruvnet/RuVector.

Merge pull request #366 from ruvnet/feat/ruvector-kalshi

feat(kalshi): ruvector-kalshi + neural-trader-strategies (ADR-151)

docs(README): Leiden refinement shipped — perfect ARI on 2-community planted SBM

RuVector commit f58f0c98f landed the Leiden community-detection
merge (Traag-et-al. 2019 three-phase: local-moves → refinement →
aggregate). Measured on a hand-crafted 2-community planted SBM:
Leiden ARI = 1.000, Louvain ARI = 0.000 — direct vindication of
the refinement phase on exactly the hub-heavy SBM failure mode
that motivated it.

README changes:
  - Added Leiden to the 'What's different about it' list with the
    planted-SBM measurement.
  - Bumped the features-section test count from 73 to 87 and
    retargeted the commit reference to f58f0c98f.

Net-effect: readers landing on the README now see that community
detection — historically a research-tool handoff — is a first-class
shipped capability in Connectome OS, with a paired under-baseline
(multi-level Louvain) that demonstrates the improvement is real.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

docs(README): Part-3 table updated — 4 of 5 exotic apps now have in-tree scaffolding

RuVector commit 07cbb8d8c lands public ABIs + productized wrappers
for 4 of the 5 Part-3 exotic applications. The Part-3 table now has
a 'Scaffolding status' column recording which are done:

  ✅ Embodied fly navigation    — BodySimulator trait + StubBody
                                   (works today) + MujocoBody
                                   panic-stub (Phase-3 shape)
  ✅ In-silico circuit lesion    — LesionStudy + CandidateCut +
                                   LesionReport (AC-5 productized)
  ⏳ Cross-species transfer     — still needs mouse Tier-2 substrate
  ✅ AI safety auditing          — StructuralAudit::new(&conn, stim).run()
  ✅ Research-paper substrate   — was already open

Downstream code can type against the new APIs today against the
deterministic default implementations and swap the production
piece in later. The 'Blocker to full' column distinguishes what
each application still needs to go from scaffolding to production.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

docs(README): alpha / research-preview callout near top

Blockquote callout placed between the opening tagline + positioning
paragraph and the bullet-list of links. Sets reader expectations
before they click into the source tree.

Content of the callout:
  - State: alpha / research preview, branch-based development
  - What ships: Tier-1 fly-scale demonstrator (73 tests green)
  - What's named-but-not-shipped: production crates
    (ruvector-connectome / ruvector-lif / ruvector-embodiment),
    real FlyWire v783 ingest, MuJoCo embodiment, Tier-2 mouse
    substrate
  - API stability: may change
  - Integrity pointer: ADR-154 §17 records the 13 honest findings
  - Use guidance: research + internal tooling OK; external
    contracts not yet advisable

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

docs(README): organize applications into 3 tables + rewrite Introduction

Two edits for readability:

1. Applications table split into three parts by tier so a reader can
   jump straight to what matches their situation:
     Part 1 — Practical (works today on the shipped demo)
     Part 2 — Semi-practical (API wrappers or corpus prep, no new research)
     Part 3 — Exotic (needs Phase-2 or Phase-3 scaffolding — each row
             now names its specific blocker in a new 'Blocker' column)
   Followed by the use-or-don't-use rule of thumb.

2. Introduction restructured from academic three-subsection layout
   (Problem → Positioning → Why now) to a reader-first progression:
     (a) Lede sentence stating what the runtime is + the
         differentiating claim, in a single line.
     (b) 'Who this is for' — a bulleted list of concrete audiences,
         replacing the abstract 'the problem' framing. Lets someone
         unfamiliar with the domain self-identify in ≤ 30 seconds.
     (c) 'The gap this closes' — the two-outcome framing (dynamics OR
         structure, never both together), with the 2024 Nature
         reference tightened to one paragraph.
     (d) 'What's different about it' — four bullet points naming
         the shipped capabilities that materially distinguish
         Connectome OS from Brian2/NEST/etc., each with its
         measured number.
     (e) 'The positioning, in one paragraph' — condenses the prior
         two-paragraph positioning section into a single paragraph
         anchored on the 'control, not scale' framing. Closes with
         a pointer back to the Applications table.
     (f) 'Why now' — unchanged three-item list.

Net effect: introduction is ~50 words longer but roughly half the
time-to-useful-signal for a first-time reader. Applications table
is the same 14 applications but grouped so readers can triage
their own match quality against the three tiers.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

docs(README): add 'Applications — practical to exotic' table near top

14 rows spanning the spectrum from 'works on the shipped demo today'
through 'needs Phase 2/3 work to finish first':

  Practical (shipped):
    1. FlyWire exploration & audit
    2. Community-detection ground truth
    3. Reference LIF for spiking-simulator benchmarks
    4. Connectome-constrained coursework
    5. Fragility monitoring on recorded spike data

  Semi-practical (API wrappers / corpus prep, no new research):
    6. Causal-perturbation tests on trained RNNs
    7. Graph-constrained RL policy priors
    8. Neural-architecture pruning with proof
    9. Neuromorphic hardware verification

  Exotic (needs Phase 2/3 work):
    10. Embodied fly navigation in VR (needs MuJoCo + NeuroMechFly)
    11. In-silico circuit-lesion studies
    12. Cross-species connectome transfer (needs mouse Tier-2)
    13. Connectome-grounded AI safety auditing
    14. Substrate for structural-intelligence research papers

Closes with a use-or-don't-use rule of thumb so readers can
self-select: yes if 'structure is the thing you want to reason
about'; no if general-purpose AI reasoning or statistical learning.

Table sits between the TOC and the Introduction so a reader who
lands on the repo can see applicability before the full framing
discussion.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

initial: Connectome OS — README + project framing

A debugging and control layer for embodied graph systems whose
structure is knowable (the connectome) rather than learned.

Source code lives at ruvnet/RuVector @ research/connectome-ruvector.
This repo is the public landing + user-facing documentation.

README sections:
  - Introduction (problem, positioning, why now)
  - Features (6 first-class capabilities + infrastructure)
  - Capabilities & comparison (vs Brian2/NEST/Auryn/GeNN/HCP/CEBRA)
  - Quick start (clone + cargo test + run_demo)
  - User guide (running sims, analyzing spikes, causal perturbation)
  - Fly-AI guide (complete FlyWire → running simulation workflow)
  - Measurement-driven findings (13 discoveries preserved in ADR §17)
  - What Connectome OS is NOT (positioning non-goals)

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

commit.message:
docs: link README screenshot + URL to live GitHub Pages dashboard

The dashboard is now published at https://ruvnet.github.io/Connectome-OS/
via the `gh-pages` branch (static Vite build of examples/connectome-fly/ui
with VITE_BASE=/Connectome-OS/).

Changes to README:

- Added a call-to-action line above the screenshot:
  "Open the live dashboard → ruvnet.github.io/Connectome-OS"
  with a note that the "engine=rust-lif" banner only goes green
  when the local Rust backend is running (Pages is static-only).
- Wrapped the screenshot in a click-through link to the same URL.
- Caption text rewritten to say "click to open the live dashboard"
  rather than assuming a local screenshot.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

docs: simple-language rewrite + full 30-discovery refresh

Top-of-README rewritten in plain language for readers who aren't
already inside the Rust/LIF/ARI vocabulary:

- Screenshot caption now opens with "A real fly brain is running
  inside your laptop" and explains the witness counter as a random
  number that changes on restart — concrete test, no jargon.
- "OS in the Linux sense" paragraph replaced with a four-step
  description of what the system actually does (runs forward,
  watches structure, cuts connections, measures change) and an
  explicit disclaimer of emergence / consciousness / uploads / AGI.
- Alpha callout flattened: what ships, what doesn't, the one
  known rough edge (Fiedler at scale), and the current CPM ceiling
  of 0.671 with the 1.12× gap to the 0.75 SOTA target.

Body updates:

- Features table: 7 capabilities (was 6), now includes the live
  Rust→browser UI with the "how to tell it's real" proof.
- Additional infrastructure: both FlyWire loaders (TSV + Princeton
  CSV) called out; 115,151-neuron dataset ships under assets/.
- Feasibility tiers: Tier 1 is "shipped, real data" not "shipped"
  + fixture-tested.
- Fly-AI "what's missing" table: FlyWire ingest and live UI move
  from ⏳ to ✅; active-subset Fiedler is the new ⏳.
- Measurement-driven findings: rows 21–30 added with one-line
  summaries and lessons; closing narrative updated — 9/15 named
  levers ruled out, the 0.425→0.671 jump was 58 % from grid
  resolution alone, and three remaining levers named concretely.

No factual changes to existing rows. All claims are tied to the
commit that produced them.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

docs: real 115k-neuron fly brain runs now — README + screenshot update

- Hero screenshot swapped to the 115,151-neuron FlyWire Princeton run
  (was: the 1024-neuron synthetic demo).
- Tagline caption updated: "n=115,151 syn=2,676,592 witness=…" banner,
  6 M real spikes in a few seconds.
- Added a plain-language "In plain language" paragraph — "a real fly
  brain is running inside your laptop right now".
- New Quick-start subsection: "Run the real fly brain in your browser"
  with build + env-var + npm commands, a measured throughput table
  comparing Fiedler-on vs Fiedler-off at 1024 vs 115k neurons, and a
  simple "how to tell it's real, not a mock" paragraph.
- Alpha callout: "26 discoveries" → "30 discoveries"; mentions the
  new CPM ceiling 0.671 at (N=512, m=19, hub=1, γ=4.4), gap 1.12×;
  acknowledges the Fiedler-at-scale open item honestly.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

docs: tighten intro language

- Tagline: "debugging and control layer for embodied graph systems
  whose structure is knowable — the connectome — rather than learned"
  → "…for neural circuits whose wiring is read off a map, not inferred
  from gradients." Same claim, half the words, punchier contrast.
- Screenshot caption: drop "the browser is downstream of …" mouthful;
  "advances into the hundreds-of-thousands of real spikes" → "crosses
  ~10⁵ real spikes". Kept the witness-counter proof intact.
- "Linux sense" paragraph: three "Not X" clauses collapsed into one;
  replaced "mystical claim about emergent mind" (too defensive) with a
  direct rejection of the three specific confusions (emergence,
  uploads, AGI).
- Alpha callout: removed narrative connectives ("Connectome OS is in
  active development on the…", "The Tier-1 fly-scale demonstrator
  (examples/connectome-fly/) is shipped and all 97 tests pass"),
  reflowed into tight clauses; now reads as a status ledger, not prose.

No factual changes — same numbers, same links, same caveats.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

feat(ui): live Rust-backed demo + README screenshot

The UI is now a live view of the real LIF engine. The previous Web-
Worker synthetic simulator is gone; every spike, every λ₂ value, every
community snapshot comes from the Rust backend
(examples/connectome-fly/src/bin/ui_server.rs) over SSE.

Proof in the screenshot:
- Top-bar engine tag: rust-lif crate=0.1.0 n=1024 modules=70 witness=N
- The witness is a per-process boot counter — no static mock can match
- window._real_spikes_total climbs into 100s of thousands in ~5s
- Validated with agent-browser: zero console errors across full tour

Also updated:
- "95 tests" → "97 tests" (current branch head)
- "20 measurement-driven discoveries" → "26"
- Narrative updated for the N=512/modules=20/γ=4.0 CPM ceiling
  (0.599 full-ARI, 1.25× gap to 0.75 SOTA — down from 1.76× at N=1024)

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

docs(README): sync to commit cfdcb8bb1 — 20 discoveries, 4 wins, CPM ranking landed

Connectome-OS README was stale: cited commit f58f0c98f (13
discoveries, 87 tests) while the upstream research branch had
moved through 7 more commits with 7 new discoveries and a 4th
unambiguous optimization win.

Updates:

  - Alpha callout: 73 → 95 tests, 13 → 20 discoveries.
  - Features list: 'shipped Leiden refinement' → 'shipped TWO
    community-detection algorithms (modularity-Leiden and
    weight-normalized CPM-Leiden)' plus the 3.97× CPM-vs-
    modularity full-partition-ARI win on the default SBM.
    Added a new bullet for the greedy-beats-Leiden 'more
    algorithm is worse on hub-heavy SBMs' surprise (discovery
    #20).
  - Features commit pointer: f58f0c98f → cfdcb8bb1.
  - Findings table grew 13 → 20 rows with new entries:
      #14 Leiden ARI=1.000 planted-SBM
      #15 bucket sort ≠ cross-path bit-exact
      #16 naive CPM collapses
      #17 weight-normalized CPM works (perfect planted)
      #18 full-partition ARI reveals 3.7× CPM win
      #19 fine-γ peak lifts to 0.425
      #20 greedy level-1 beats Leiden on full-ARI
  - Insight paragraph extended: three wins now fit the
    orthogonal-axis rule (6, 14, 17); items 18 and 20 added a
    new lesson — a test's metric choice can hide the real
    ranking.

No algorithmic claim changes without evidence; every new row has
a measured number + a commit reference.

commit.message:
fix(workspace): unblock --no-default-features build on Windows (#366, #415) (#425)

mat, sensing-server, and train all depended on signal with default features
enabled, which pulled ndarray-linalg → openblas-src → vcpkg/system-BLAS through
the entire workspace. --no-default-features at the workspace root could not
opt out of BLAS, breaking cargo build / cargo test on Windows without vcpkg.

Set default-features = false on the signal dep in all three consumers so the
flag actually propagates. Also gate signal::ruvsense::field_model::tests
::test_estimate_occupancy_noise_only with #[cfg(feature = "eigenvalue")] —
the test unwraps a NotCalibrated stub when eigenvalue is compiled out.

Validated: cargo test --workspace --no-default-features → 1,538 passed,
0 failed, 8 ignored. ESP32-S3 on COM7 still streams live CSI.

commit.message:
Add Examples section to root README — index across Rust/Python/Node/WASM/GPU

Brief table of the 14 examples under examples/ with links per stack and
a short callout of the highest-leverage example in each. Points readers
at examples/README.md for the full cross-language story and the witness
ground-truth (dea58c64...07d7e4) that every implementation reproduces
byte-exactly.

Inserted between User Guide and Status — the natural spot for "okay,
how do I see this run end-to-end" after the Quick start + How it works
+ User guide reading flow.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

Verify + harden cross-language examples; add GPU/CUDA examples + SDK ADRs

Verification swarm — three security-auditor agents (Python, Node, WASM) plus
a fourth GPU-examples agent — independently ran every test suite, ran the
CLIs against a real Rust-produced sidecar, audited DoS caps / dep CVEs /
trust boundaries, and applied fixes in-place.

Verifier verdicts:
  Python  MOSTLY-OK-WITH-FIXES → fixed → all-green (62 + 1 gated)
  Node    MOSTLY-OK-WITH-FIXES → fixed → all-green (43 + 6 fixture-gated)
  WASM    PRODUCTION-READY (3 low-priority docs/CSP nits applied)
  GPU     New examples added; built clean; 5/5 tests; 38× / 21× speedup

Security fixes applied:

  Python (3 Medium):
  - examples/python/02-bundle-server/publish.py — symlink-traversal:
    a symlink under the publish root let publish() write outside the
    root. Now resolve()s both target and root, asserts containment via
    is_relative_to, and rejects newline / NUL / control-byte components
    in the key (log-injection guard).
  - examples/python/04-rag-grounded/pipeline.py::_resolve_data_file —
    docstring claimed containment but didn't enforce it. A bundle with
    data_ref="file:///etc/passwd" or data_filename="../../../etc/hostname"
    would be opened. All three resolution branches now resolve() and
    require containment under bundle_dir; non-regular files (FIFOs,
    devices) refused.
  - examples/python/04-rag-grounded/pipeline.py::read_ruvec1 — slurped
    entire file before bounds-checking, OOM on hostile oversized file.
    Now streams: read 24-byte header, validate, fstat-cross-check size
    against header math, then read the bounded payload.
  - tests/test_pipeline.py — error-message wording assertion adjusted to
    match the new fstat-based check ("file size" vs "payload size").

  Node (2 High + 2 Medium, fixed by verifier in-place):
  - 02-bundle-publisher/src/watcher.ts — pre-read st.size > MAX_BUNDLE_BYTES
    check; without it, a 10 GB attacker file under the watch root would
    be readFileSync'd before parseBundle's cap fired.
  - 04-mcp-tool/src/snapshot.ts — pre-read size caps on bundle JSON +
    index.rbpx; isFile() check rejects FIFOs/dirs/device nodes; refuses
    untrusted MCP-client-supplied paths that point at hostile files.
  - 04-mcp-tool/src/server.ts — LRU bound on snapshotCache (16 entries);
    unbounded Map of RbpxItem[] was a memory leak under adversarial use.
  - 03-subprocess-wrapper/src/rulake.ts — bounded child stdout/stderr
    buffer at 16 MiB with truncate-and-SIGKILL on overflow; a misbehaving
    binary that flooded the pipe could grow the parent heap.
  - 01-verify-witness/tsconfig.json — rootDir/include collision broke
    `npm run build`; rootDir set to ".", explicit excludes added.
  - All four package.json — vitest exclude flags for build artifacts.
  - 04-mcp-tool/tests/server.test.ts — fixture-shape assertions relaxed
    to match the README-recommended sidecar_daemon emission.

  WASM (3 Low — docs/defense-in-depth):
  - 01-witness-verifier-browser/index.html — Content-Security-Policy
    meta tag (defense-in-depth; default-src 'self', script-src 'self'
    'wasm-unsafe-eval', object-src 'none', form-action 'none'). No XSS
    surface today (no innerHTML, no eval, no CDN scripts), but the page
    is a copy-paste reference for downstream users.
  - 03-bundle-toolkit-cli/README.md — explicit "Path inputs" section
    documenting that path-arg length is bounded by WASI runtime + kernel
    PATH_MAX, and that the WASI capability model (preopened-dir) is the
    actual reachability boundary.
  - wasm/README.md — "Skipped optional tooling" section explaining
    when wasm-opt / cargo-audit / wasmtime are required vs optional.

GPU/CUDA examples (new, examples/gpu/):

  01-cuda-brute-force/  — cudarc 0.19 + nvcc 13.0 + CUDA C kernel.
                          Witness-verified, brute-force L2 on RTX 5080.
                          Measured: 100k×128 → CPU 5.05ms, GPU kernel
                          0.13ms (38.6×); 500k×256 → CPU 48.2ms, GPU
                          1.7ms (28.5×). Top-K agreement 100%.
                          4/4 tests pass.
  02-wgpu-portable/     — wgpu 29 + WGSL compute shader. Cross-platform
                          GPU (Vulkan/Metal/DX12, no CUDA toolkit).
                          ~21× kernel-only speedup; 1/1 test passes.
  03-rabitq-gpu-design-note/ — Markdown design note for the missing
                          rabitq GPU port: per-candidate __popc kernel,
                          AoS↔SoA layout question, VectorKernel trait
                          integration shape, recall guarantees, witness
                          compatibility argument, 7-step implementation
                          checklist for shipping ruvector-rabitq-cuda.

  Honest framing in every README: ruLake's RaBitQ compressed-scan
  kernel does NOT have a GPU port today (ADR-157 is scaffolding-only).
  These examples take the verify-then-brute-force-floats path. Module 03
  is the explicit bridge.

  Each GPU crate is standalone (empty [workspace]) — neither leaks
  cudarc / wgpu deps into the host workspace.

SDK ADRs (proposed-status, drafted by the Node verifier):
  docs/adrs/sdk/ADR-002-python-sdk.md       (531 lines) — pyo3, NumPy
                                                          zero-copy, ABI3
  docs/adrs/sdk/ADR-003-nodejs-typescript-sdk.md (576 lines) — napi-rs,
                                                          Float32Array
                                                          zero-copy, ESM

  These document the FFI/async/packaging shape for future Python and Node
  bindings, before any code lands. Both inherit ADR-001's submodule +
  no-root-workspace discipline.

Independent verification:
  All Python tests (62 passing + 1 env-gated) — re-run after fixes, green.
  All Node tests (43 passing) + 6 fixture-gated — re-run with /tmp/rulake-fixture
  generated by a one-shot Rust example, all 49 green.
  WASM CLI vs committed Rust fixture — witness dea58c64...07d7e4 byte-exact.
  GPU CLIs vs committed fixtures — same witness, same byte-exact agreement.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

Add cross-language examples — Python, Node TS, Rust WASM (3-agent swarm)

Eleven runnable example modules across three target environments, each
with its own README, install instructions, tests, and a real
runnable-from-clean-checkout flow. 88 files, ~19,700 lines.

Produced by a 3-agent swarm working in parallel. Each agent received the
exact bundle JSON schema and SHAKE-256 witness algorithm from
src/bundle.rs (length-prefixed, domain-separated, with the audit-driven
Generation tag byte) and verified its implementation against a real
Rust-produced sidecar from `cargo run --release --example sidecar_daemon`.
All three reported byte-exact agreement on the witness output:
witness `31c0865f078d9d646edaf0fe339d5d8c20a04bac9e95571fae91035306c2b584`
reproduces identically in Python, Node, browser WASM, Node WASM, and the
WASI CLI.

Layout:
  examples/python/                4 modules — verify-witness, bundle-server,
                                  subprocess-wrapper, rag-grounded
                                  (62/63 tests pass, 1 skipped behind an
                                  env flag for hermetic CI)
  examples/nodejs/                4 modules — verify-witness, bundle-publisher,
                                  subprocess-wrapper, mcp-tool
                                  (43/43 tests pass; mcp-tool exposes
                                  rulake_search / rulake_verify_witness /
                                  rulake_bundle_info as MCP-callable tools)
  examples/wasm/                  3 modules — browser verifier (~127 KB),
                                  Node verifier (~127 KB), WASI CLI (~315 KB).
                                  Each is a standalone Cargo package with
                                  empty [workspace] so wasm32 builds don't
                                  pull in the host crate's std::sync / rayon.
  examples/README.md              Top-level index + cross-language story
  examples/{python,nodejs,wasm}/.gitignore
                                  Per-tree build-artifact exclusions
                                  (.venv/, node_modules/, pkg/, target/)

Cross-cutting design choices the swarm converged on (worth noting since
they apply to any future ruLake client in any language):

- Witness as cache key — every example treats the SHAKE-256 hex string as
  the canonical content-address for a bundle. Same witness → same bytes →
  same retrieval results across processes and languages.
- DoS caps enforced at every parser boundary — bundle ≤ 64 KiB, fields
  ≤ 4 KiB, witness exactly 64 hex chars. Refuse format_version > 2.
- Collision regression pinned in every test suite: Num(7) and
  Opaque("\x07\0\0\0\0\0\0\0") MUST produce different witnesses. If they
  match, the Generation variant tag byte is missing from the witness
  computation.
- No FFI — none of the examples wrap ruLake via pyo3 / napi-rs /
  wasm-bindgen of the full crate. The bundle protocol is the
  language-portable primitive that makes cross-language usage cheap.

Notable swarm gotchas resolved during the run:

- Host RUSTFLAGS=-C link-arg=-fuse-ld=mold breaks the wasm32 linker.
  Each WASM build.sh now `unset`s RUSTFLAGS first.
- @noble/hashes/sha3 ESM export is `shake256`, not `shake_256`. Worth
  pinning in any future "use SHAKE-256 in JS" reference.
- @modelcontextprotocol/sdk's registerTool() takes Zod schemas; pulled in
  zod for the mcp-tool example.
- Module 4 (RAG) consumes the documented `ruvec1` data file from
  FsBackend rather than the rabitq-encoded `index.rbpx` snapshot, since
  there's no Python rabitq decoder. Same content-addressed substrate
  story; brute-force L2 instead of compressed scan. README spells this
  out explicitly.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

Add vertical-applications research swarm — agentic, AI/ML, edge, exotic

Four white-paper-voice notes under docs/research/ exploring where ruLake's
substrate primitives (witness chain, three-mode consistency, atomic bundle
protocol, warm-restart, federated rerank) earn their keep across vertical
domains. ~7,000 lines total.

Produced by a 4-agent research swarm spawned in parallel. Each agent
received the same "what ruLake actually is" capability summary derived
from docs/review/, plus a narrow domain prompt — so claims stay sourced
and the README oversell pattern the deep review surfaced is not repeated.

Notes:
  agentic.md  (2,073 lines) — agent memory hierarchies (ADR-156),
                              multi-agent swarms with shared world model,
                              tool-using retrieval (MCP), long-running
                              autonomous agents, ReasoningBank-style replay
  ai-ml.md    (2,111 lines) — production RAG with witness-pinned provenance,
                              multi-tenant SaaS RAG, lakehouse cache,
                              embedding-pipeline cache, vector-aware feature
                              store, recsys, training-data dedup
  edge.md     (1,848 lines) — on-device LLM RAG, browser/WASM, robotics,
                              IIoT, federated edge with cloud truth, mobile
                              offline-first, Pi/Jetson-class, in-vehicle
  exotic.md   (1,039 lines) — healthcare CDS audit, financial fraud +
                              regulatory lineage, climate/earth observation,
                              genomics, robotics fleet learning, scientific
                              reproducibility, smart cities, spaceflight
  README.md                 — index + cross-cutting findings + how-to-use

Cross-cutting agreement across all four notes:

- The witness chain is the differentiator, not the cache itself. Faster
  vector retrieval is commodity; cryptographically-anchored, content-
  addressed, snapshot-replayable, publication-portable provenance is not.
- Consistency::Frozen + warm_from_dir is the regulated-vertical killer
  feature. Healthcare audit, financial replay, scientific reproducibility,
  spaceflight reference data — same primitive, four domains.
- Cross-process witness sharing matters at swarm and fleet scale; verified
  by tests/federation_smoke.rs::two_backends_share_cache_when_witness_matches.

Cross-cutting gaps the swarm called out (all already in docs/review/, but
restated per-vertical with specific implementation impact):

- No production cloud backends ship today (M2+ roadmap)
- Governance plane unimplemented (RBAC, PII, OpenLineage are M4)
- Single-writer per backend; bundle protocol assumes one publisher
- Consistency is per-RuLake, not per-collection
- Sharded CacheState is the next concurrent-QPS ceiling (perf review B1)
- No NEON / WASM kernels in ruvector-rabitq

Methodology note: the exotic agent stalled twice before writing
(stream-idle timeout). exotic.md was authored directly in the orchestrator
turn against the same context brief — same source, same ground rules,
shorter than the three parallel-produced notes (~1,000 vs ~1,800-2,100
lines) but covering 8 verticals with the same "concrete implementation
outline + honest gaps" structure.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

Fix Dockerfile — switch to rust:bookworm + buildkit cache mounts

Two issues turned up running the build end-to-end:

1. rust:1.83-bookworm SIGILLs on this host even on a trivial `cargo build`
   of `serde`. Confirmed not a CPU-flag issue (host is Ryzen 9 9950X with
   AVX-512); rust:1.82 and rust:1.85 work fine. Image-specific defect on
   1.83. Switched to `rust:bookworm` (currently 1.95) for headroom.

2. ruvector-rabitq imports AVX-512 stdarch intrinsics that only became
   stable after Rust 1.89, so any image older than 1.89 fails compilation
   regardless of host CPU. Bumped Cargo.toml's `rust-version` from 1.77
   to 1.89 to match what actually builds, and noted the constraint in the
   Dockerfile comment so future image bumps know not to regress below it.

3. The dep-prebuild placeholder-source trick was leaving stale lib
   metadata that broke the real build. Replaced with BuildKit cache
   mounts (`--mount=type=cache`) for `/usr/local/cargo/registry` and
   `/build/target`, which is the conventional Rust-on-Docker pattern.
   Cleaner Dockerfile, properly cached repeat builds.

Verified end-to-end:
    docker build -t rulake .                → builds in ~90 s cold
    docker run --rm rulake --fast           → tax=1.01× @ n=5000
    cargo build --release                   → still works on host
    cargo test --release                    → 22/22 federation tests pass

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

Make repo standalone-buildable — submodule, install.sh, Docker, ADR-001

Closes the biggest gap from docs/review/capabilities.md: a fresh clone
of just this repo would not build. The imported Cargo.toml inherited
version/edition/license/authors/repository plus serde/thiserror/rand/
rand_distr/rayon from the upstream RuVector workspace, and depended on
ruvector-rabitq via a path that walked out of the repo.

Standalone-ization (governed by docs/adrs/ADR-001-standalone-repo-strategy.md):

- Vendor RuVector as a git submodule under vendor/ruvector. ruLake's
  Cargo.toml now points ruvector-rabitq at vendor/ruvector/crates/
  ruvector-rabitq, and rabitq's *.workspace = true keys resolve against
  the submodule's own Cargo.toml. No copy-paste of kernel code.
- Drop the [workspace] table from the root Cargo.toml entirely. With a
  root workspace cargo absorbed rabitq into our workspace and failed
  on the inheritance lookups; with no root workspace, rabitq finds its
  upstream workspace via path-walk and ruLake builds as a single package.
- Pin every formerly-workspace dependency to a concrete version that
  matches the upstream pin (rand 0.8, rand_distr 0.4, rayon 1.10,
  serde 1.0, thiserror 2.0, serde_json 1.0). No double-compiles.
- Add Cargo.lock so binary builds (rulake-demo) are reproducible.

Verified locally:
    cargo build                                 → ok in 3.6 s
    cargo test --release                        → 21 unit + 22 integration pass
    cargo run --release --bin rulake-demo --fast → 1.00× tax @ n=5000
    cargo run --release --example sidecar_daemon → coherent through rotation
    cargo run --release --example warm_restart  → byte-exact warm restart

Onboarding paths (added in this commit):

- install.sh — toolchain check, submodule init, build, test, smoke-demo.
  Idempotent. Three skip-knobs for CI variants.
- Dockerfile — multi-stage rust:1.83-bookworm → debian:bookworm-slim,
  ENTRYPOINT is rulake-demo so `docker run --rm rulake --fast` works.
- .dockerignore — keeps target/, .git/, docs/ out of the build context.
- .gitignore — standard Rust + .claude-flow/ + local rulake snapshot dirs.

README updates restricted to factual corrections, no claim weakening:

- "83 tests passing" → "43 tests passing (21 unit + 22 integration)"
  (matches `cargo test --release` exactly; the 83-figure was inherited
  and never sourced).
- Broken relative link `crates/ruvector-rulake/src/backend.rs` → `src/backend.rs`.
- Upstream-repo links for BENCHMARK.md, examples/, src/fs_backend.rs,
  ADRs 155–158 → repo-local equivalents now that those files live here.
- New "Build from source" + "Or run inside Docker" subsections under
  Quick start so a reader knows the supported onboarding path while
  the crate is pre-publish.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

Add deep code review — capabilities, performance, security

Three reviewer-grade documents under docs/review/ assessing the
M1 + M1.5 + persistence surface against ADR-155..158:

- capabilities.md — full public-API map, README claim matrix, M1 vs
  M2+ split, ADR conformance, dependency surface, test inventory.
- performance.md — hot-path walk-through, lock topology, allocation
  patterns, rayon usage, ranked bottlenecks, prioritized
  optimization opportunities cross-checked against BENCHMARK.md.
- security.md — threat model, severity-rated findings (0 Critical,
  0 High, 6 Medium, 6 Low, 5 Info), witness/digest scheme audit,
  path-traversal review, dependency / supply-chain review.
- README.md indexes the three with executive summaries and the
  overall verdict.

Verdict: M1 claims are mechanical, not aspirational. Witness scheme
is sound; Arc-drop-lock concurrency is the central design win;
zero unsafe in src/. Main gaps are README overselling
ruvector-rabitq-owned features as ruLake capabilities, the
standalone-build packaging gap from workspace-inheritance in
Cargo.toml, and six operator-footgun-class security hardenings.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

Add ADRs related to ruLake (155–158)

ADR-155 ruLake — Vector-Native Federation Intermediary on RVF (Accepted M1)
ADR-156 ruLake as Memory Substrate for Agent Brain Systems (Proposed)
ADR-157 Optional Accelerator Plane — VectorKernel Trait + Dispatch (Proposed)
ADR-158 Optional Rotation Kind + QVCache Positioning (Proposed)

Sourced from ruvnet/RuVector docs/adr/. 155/156 are explicitly
ruLake; 157/158 reference ruLake substantively in their bodies.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

Import ruLake source from ruvnet/RuVector@main

Mirrors crates/ruvector-rulake/ as the standalone repo source:
src/ (lake, cache, backend, bundle, fs_backend, error, lib, bin/rulake-demo),
tests/federation_smoke.rs, examples/{sidecar_daemon,warm_restart}.rs,
Cargo.toml, BENCHMARK.md.

Cargo.toml retains workspace inheritance and a path dep on
ruvector-rabitq — both will need standalone-ization before this
builds outside the RuVector workspace.

commit.message:
docs(adr): ADR-084 — promote Proposed → Accepted

All five implementation passes plus four security-review hardenings
shipped in PR #435 (squash-merged as d71ef9a). Acceptance numbers
measured on synthetic AETHER-shape data:

- Compare-cost reduction: 8x-30x floor → 43-51x pair-wise (d=512),
  12.4x top-K (d=128 n=1024 k=8), 7.6x full pipeline (d=128 n=4096 k=8).
- Top-K coverage: ≥90% floor → 90%+ at prefilter_factor=8 (78.9%
  at factor=4 documented as fail; codified in
  test_search_prefilter_topk_coverage_meets_adr_084).
- Wire envelope: 28-byte AETHER 128-d (vs 512-byte raw float; 18x
  compression).

The third acceptance criterion (`< 1 pp end-to-end accuracy regression`)
needs a real-CSI soak test against a multi-day AETHER trace; that's
post-merge follow-up rather than a merge-blocker. Synthetic-data
acceptance was sufficient evidence to ship.

PR #434 (ADR-086 firmware-side gate) merged separately as 17509a2.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

docs(adr): ADR-086 — edge novelty gate (proposed) (#434)

Pushes the ADR-084 novelty sensor down into the ESP32 sensor MCU's
Layer 4 (On-device Feature Extraction) of ADR-081's 5-layer kernel:
sketch + 32-slot ring bank in IRAM, suppress UDP send when novelty
< CONFIG_RV_EDGE_NOVELTY_THRESHOLD (default 0.05).

Wire format bumps to magic 0xC5110007 with two new fields
(suppressed_since_last: u16, gate_version: u8) packed in by narrowing
the existing 16-bit quality_flags to 8-bit (only 8 bits were ever
defined). Frame size stays at 60 bytes; v6 receivers fall back
gracefully.

Stuck-gate self-heal at CONFIG_RV_EDGE_MAX_CONSEC_SUPPRESS (default
50 frames ≈ 10 s) so a wedged threshold can't silently disappear a
node. Default-off Kconfig so existing deployments are unaffected.

Validation commitments:
- ≤ 200 µs sketch insert+score on Xtensa LX7
- ≥ 30% UDP TX-energy reduction in steady-state quiet rooms
- ≤ 5 pp drop on cluster-Pi novelty top-K coverage vs unsuppressed
- ≥ 50% bandwidth reduction in stable-room scenarios

Six-pass implementation plan, default-off Kconfig, QEMU + COM7
hardware-in-loop validation. Honest gaps flagged: Xtensa LX7 POPCNT
absence is conjecture (Pass 2 bench is the falsifier); interaction
with ADR-082's Tentative→Active gate is the likeliest weak point
(Open Q4).

ADR-087 / ADR-088 reserved as pointer stubs at end:
- ADR-087: Pass-4 mesh-exchange scope (cluster↔cluster vs sensor→Pi)
- ADR-088: Firmware-release coordination policy

Status: Proposed. SOTA review by goal-planner agent.
