commit.message:
feat(ruvector,signal,sensing-server): ADR-084 Passes 1/1.5/2/3 — RaBitQ similarity sensor implementation (#435)

* feat(ruvector): ADR-084 Pass 1 — sketch module foundation

Implements Pass 1 of ADR-084 (RaBitQ similarity sensor): a thin
RuView-flavored API over `ruvector_core::quantization::BinaryQuantized`,
exposed at `wifi_densepose_ruvector::{Sketch, SketchBank, SketchError}`.

API surface:
- `Sketch::from_embedding(&[f32], sketch_version: u16)` — sign-quantize
  a dense embedding into a 1-bit-per-dim packed sketch.
- `Sketch::distance` — hamming distance with schema-mismatch error.
- `Sketch::distance_unchecked` — hot-path variant for sketches already
  validated as same-schema.
- `SketchBank::insert/topk/novelty` — bank with caller-assigned u32 IDs,
  schema locked at first insert, novelty = min_distance / embedding_dim.

Schema versioning (`sketch_version: u16` + `embedding_dim: u16`) prevents
silent comparisons across embedding-model generations. Bumping the model
forces re-sketch of the candidate bank.

Pass 1 establishes the API and unit-test foundation. Acceptance criteria
(8x-30x compare-cost reduction, 90% top-K coverage, <1pp accuracy regression)
are measured per-site in Passes 2-5.

Validated:
- 12 new tests pass (sketch construction, hamming, top-K ordering,
  schema lock, schema rejection, novelty)
- cargo test --workspace --no-default-features → 1,551 passed, 0 failed,
  8 ignored (was 1,539 before; +12 new tests)
- ESP32-S3 on COM7 still streaming live CSI (cb #117300)

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

* bench(ruvector): ADR-084 acceptance — sketch-vs-float compare cost

Adds sketch_bench measuring the first ADR-084 acceptance criterion
(8x-30x compare cost reduction) at three dimensions and a realistic
top-K@k=8 over 1024 sketches.

Measured (Windows host, criterion --warm-up 1s --measurement 3s):

  compare_d512:
    float_l2:        197.03 ns/op
    float_cosine:    231.17 ns/op
    sketch_hamming:    4.56 ns/op  → 43-51x speedup

  topk_d128_n1024_k8:
    float_l2_topk:    47.59 us
    sketch_hamming:    6.34 us     → 7.5x speedup

Pair-wise compare exceeds the 8-30x acceptance criterion by an order
of magnitude. Top-K is at 7.5x — close to the threshold; the sort
dominates at this bank size, which is a Pass 1.5 optimization
opportunity (partial-sort heap for small K).

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

* perf(ruvector): ADR-084 Pass 1.5 — partial-sort heap in SketchBank::topk

Replace `sort_by_key + truncate` (O(n log n)) with a fixed-size max-heap
(O(n log k)) for top-K queries when n > k. Fast path when n ≤ k stays
on the simple sort.

Bench at d=128, n=1024, k=8 (Windows host, criterion 3s measurement):

  Before (sort + truncate):   6.34 µs/op
  After  (heap):              3.83 µs/op    -39.4% / +1.65× faster

Combined with the 32× memory shrink and 47.6 µs → 3.83 µs total path
saving:

  topk_d128_n1024_k8 vs float_l2_topk:
    Pass 1   sort_by_key:  47.59 µs / 6.34 µs =  7.5× speedup
    Pass 1.5 heap:         47.59 µs / 3.83 µs = 12.4× speedup

Now over the ADR-084 acceptance criterion of 8× minimum. Heap pays off
strictly more at larger n; benchmark at n=4096 is a Pass-2 follow-up.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

* feat(signal): ADR-084 Pass 2 — sketch-prefilter for EmbeddingHistory::search

Adds `EmbeddingHistory::with_sketch(...)` and `search_prefilter(query, k,
prefilter_factor)`. The prefilter sketches the query, hamming-ranks the
parallel sketch array to take the top `k * prefilter_factor` candidates,
then refines those with exact cosine and returns the top-K.

`EmbeddingHistory::new(...)` is unchanged — sketches are opt-in via the
new constructor. `search_prefilter` falls back to brute-force `search`
when sketches are disabled, so callers never see incorrect results.

ADR-084 acceptance criterion empirically validated:

  Synthetic 128-d AETHER-shape, n=256, 16 queries:
    k=8,  prefilter_factor=4 → 78.9% top-K coverage  (FAIL <90%)
    k=8,  prefilter_factor=8 → ≥90%  top-K coverage  (PASS)
    k=16, prefilter_factor=8 → ≥90%  top-K coverage  (PASS)

The factor=4 default that I'd planned in Pass 1 falls below the 90% bar
on uniform-random synthetic data. Production callers should use **8**
unless their embeddings carry enough structure (real AETHER traces
likely will) to clear the bar at lower factors. Documented in the
search_prefilter docstring and asserted in
test_search_prefilter_topk_coverage_meets_adr_084.

FIFO eviction now drains the parallel sketches array in lockstep —
test_search_prefilter_evicts_sketches_on_fifo guards against the two
arrays drifting (which would silently corrupt top-K via index
mismatch).

Validated:
- cargo test --workspace --no-default-features → 1,554 passed,
  0 failed, 8 ignored (was 1,551; +3 new prefilter tests)
- ESP32-S3 on COM7 still streaming live CSI (cb #3200)

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

* bench(signal): ADR-084 Pass 2 — end-to-end search_prefilter speedup

Measures EmbeddingHistory::search_prefilter (sketch + cosine refine)
vs the brute-force EmbeddingHistory::search baseline at three realistic
AETHER bank sizes, with the empirically validated prefilter_factor=8.

Measured (Windows host, criterion --warm-up 1s --measurement 3s):

  d=128, k=8:
    n=256   brute_force_cosine = 31.98 us, prefilter = 13.78 us → 2.3x
    n=1024  brute_force_cosine = 110.4 us, prefilter = 16.64 us → 6.6x
    n=4096  brute_force_cosine = 507.4 us, prefilter = 66.37 us → 7.6x

Speedup grows with bank size (sketch overhead is fixed; brute-force
scales linearly with n). At n=4k the prefilter approaches the 8x
ADR-084 acceptance criterion; at n=10k+ (realistic multi-day
deployment banks) it crosses cleanly. Below n=512 the brute-force
path is already cheap (sub-50 us) so the prefilter's narrower wins
don't materially affect the hot path.

Coverage acceptance (≥90% top-K agreement) is exercised in the
unit-test suite, not the bench. The bench measures cost only.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

* feat(signal): ADR-084 Pass 3 — EmbeddingHistory::novelty primitive

Adds the cluster-Pi novelty-sensor primitive: `EmbeddingHistory::novelty(query)`
returns `Option<f32>` in [0.0, 1.0] where 0.0 = exact-match-in-bank
and 1.0 = no-overlap. Returns None when sketches are disabled so
callers can fall back gracefully (existing `EmbeddingHistory::new`
constructor stays sketch-disabled).

This is the building block of the cluster-Pi novelty gate
described in ADR-084 §"cluster-Pi novelty sensor": each sensor node
maintains a bank of recent feature vectors, the gate scores the
incoming frame's novelty against the bank, and the heavy CNN /
pose-model wake gate consumes the score.

Wiring novelty into sensing-server's NodeState happens in a
follow-up — that's a ~50-line surgical change touching main.rs that
deserves its own commit. This patch lands the primitive + tests so
the wiring is straightforward.

Three regression tests added:
- test_novelty_returns_none_without_sketches
  (graceful fallback when bank is sketch-less)
- test_novelty_zero_for_exact_match_one_for_empty_bank
  (semantic boundaries)
- test_novelty_decreases_as_bank_grows_around_query
  (gradient direction — guards against reversed comparator)

Validated:
- cargo test --workspace --no-default-features → 1,557 passed,
  0 failed, 8 ignored (was 1,554; +3 new novelty tests)
- ESP32-S3 on COM7 still streaming live CSI (cb #7600)

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

* feat(sensing-server): ADR-084 Pass 3 — wire novelty into NodeState

Wires the EmbeddingHistory::novelty primitive (Pass 3 prior commit)
into the per-node frame ingestion path on the cluster Pi. Each
incoming CSI frame now updates a per-node sketch bank of the last
6.4 s of feature vectors and produces a novelty score in [0.0, 1.0]
that downstream model-wake gates can consume.

Two NodeState structs were touched (one in types.rs and a
refactoring-leftover duplicate in main.rs that the call site uses);
both gain feature_history + last_novelty_score fields and an
update_novelty helper that:
- truncates / zero-pads incoming amplitudes to NOVELTY_VECTOR_DIM (56)
- scores novelty *before* inserting (so a frame doesn't see itself)
- FIFO-evicts when the bank reaches NOVELTY_HISTORY_CAPACITY (64)

Wired at the per-node ESP32 frame path in main.rs:3772 (immediately
before frame_history.push_back). Existing call sites that operate on
the singleton SensingState (not per-node) intentionally untouched —
they will be wired in a follow-up alongside the WebSocket update
envelope's novelty_score field.

Two new unit tests in novelty_tests:
- first_frame_yields_max_novelty_then_zero_on_repeat
  (semantic boundaries: empty bank = 1.0, exact repeat = 0.0)
- handles_short_and_long_amplitude_vectors
  (truncate / zero-pad robustness across hardware variants)

Validated:
- cargo test --workspace --no-default-features → 1,559 passed,
  0 failed, 8 ignored (was 1,557; +2 new novelty tests)
- ESP32-S3 on COM7 still streaming live CSI (cb #3900)

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

* hardening(ruvector): L2 from PR #435 review — overflow on >u16::MAX dims

Pass 1.6 hardening, addressing L2 finding from the security review on
PR #435 (https://github.com/ruvnet/RuView/pull/435#issuecomment-4321285519):

The original `Sketch::from_embedding` used `debug_assert!` for the
`embedding.len() <= u16::MAX` invariant, which compiled out in release
builds. A caller passing a 65,536+ -dim embedding would silently
truncate the dimension count via `as u16` cast — two over-long inputs
would then compare as same-dimensional rather than as 64k vs 70k, and
the dimension confusion would not surface anywhere.

Two-part fix:
- `from_embedding` (infallible) now SATURATES `embedding_dim` to
  `u16::MAX` rather than truncating. Two over-long inputs still get
  packed bit-correctly by `BinaryQuantized` and the saturated dim is
  consistent across both, so they compare predictably (just with an
  upper-bounded distance).
- `try_from_embedding` (new, fallible) returns
  `Err(SketchError::EmbeddingDimOverflow{got, max})` when the input
  exceeds `u16::MAX`. Use this when an over-long input should fail
  loudly rather than be silently saturated.
- New error variant `SketchError::EmbeddingDimOverflow` with the
  observed `got` and the `max` (`u16::MAX as usize`).
- New regression test `try_from_embedding_rejects_over_long_input`
  asserts both paths: try_ → Err, infallible → saturate.

Validated:
- 13 sketch unit tests pass (was 12; +1 for L2 boundary).
- cargo test --workspace --no-default-features → 1,560 passed,
  0 failed, 8 ignored (was 1,559; +1).
- ESP32-S3 on COM7 streaming live CSI (cb #100, fresh boot RSSI -48 dBm).

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

* hardening(ruvector,signal): L1+L3 from PR #435 review

Two follow-ups to the security review on PR #435:

L1 — Defensive `if let Some(...)` for SketchBank::topk heap peek.
The original `.expect("heap len == k > 0")` was mathematically
unreachable (k > 0 enforced at function entry, heap.len() >= k branch
guards), but a structural pattern makes the impossibility a type
property rather than a runtime invariant. Same hot-path cost; zero
panic risk in the production binary.

L3 — Guard `embedding_dim == 0` in `EmbeddingHistory::novelty`.
A 0-dim history is constructible via `with_sketch(0, ...)`; without
the guard the function returned `NaN` (min_d as f32 / 0.0), silently
poisoning every downstream gate (model-wake, anomaly-emit, etc).
Now returns Some(1.0) — fail-loud at "no comparison possible →
maximally novel," never NaN. New regression test
`test_novelty_zero_dim_history_returns_one_not_nan` pins it down.

Validated:
- cargo test --workspace --no-default-features → 1,561 passed,
  0 failed, 8 ignored (was 1,560; +1 for the L3 NaN guard test).
- ESP32-S3 on COM7 streaming live CSI (cb #12400, RSSI fresh).

L4 (f64→f32 cast) is documentation-only and lands in a follow-up
patch; L8 (always-on novelty sensor) is an observation, not a fix.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

* feat(sensing-server): ADR-084 Pass 3.5 — novelty_score on PerNodeFeatureInfo

Adds an optional `novelty_score: Option<f32>` field to
PerNodeFeatureInfo, the per-node WebSocket envelope shape. Mirrored
on both struct definitions (types.rs canonical + main.rs's
refactoring-leftover duplicate) so the schema is consistent.

`#[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]` keeps existing
WebSocket consumers unaffected — old clients see no extra field
unless the server populates it. No PerNodeFeatureInfo literal
construction sites exist today (all `node_features: None`), so this
is a schema-only addition; live population from
`NodeState::last_novelty_score` lands in a Pass 3.6 follow-up that
also wires `node_features: Some(...)` at the per-node ESP32 frame
emit path.

Validated:
- cargo test --workspace --no-default-features → 1,561 passed,
  0 failed, 8 ignored (no change; schema-only).
- ESP32-S3 on COM7 streaming live CSI (cb #2100, fresh boot).

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

* feat(sensing-server): ADR-084 Pass 3.6 — populate node_features with novelty_score

Wires `node_features: Some(...)` at the two per-node ESP32 frame
emit sites (formerly `node_features: None`). Adds a `build_node_features`
helper that constructs `Vec<PerNodeFeatureInfo>` from `s.node_states`,
including the per-node `last_novelty_score`.

This completes the Pass 3.x track — novelty score now flows from
NodeState → PerNodeFeatureInfo → SensingUpdate envelope → WebSocket
clients. Cluster-Pi UI / model-wake / anomaly-emit gates can read
it without round-tripping back to the server.

Three other call sites (singleton paths at 1772, 1911, 4170) keep
`node_features: None` for now — those are for the offline /
simulated paths that don't have per-node ESP32 state. They'll get
populated when their parent flows wire up real multi-node fanout.

Stale flag uses `ESP32_OFFLINE_TIMEOUT` (5s) — same threshold the
rest of the system uses to decide a node has dropped.

Validated:
- cargo test --workspace --no-default-features → 1,561 passed,
  0 failed, 8 ignored (no change; integration test would be wire-
  format diff in a follow-up).
- ESP32-S3 on COM7 streaming live CSI (cb #100, fresh boot,
  RSSI -49 dBm).

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

* feat(ruvector): ADR-084 Pass 4 — WireSketch wire-format primitive

Adds `WireSketch::serialize` / `deserialize` for transmitting a
sketch + novelty score over any byte-stream channel — cluster↔cluster
mesh (ADR-066 swarm bridge when it exists), sensor→cluster-Pi UDP
(ADR-086 edge gate complement), gateway→cloud QUIC. Channel-agnostic
by design.

Wire layout (12-byte header + ceil(dim/8) bytes payload, little-endian):

  [0..4]   magic = 0xC5110084
  [4..6]   format_version = 1
  [6..8]   sketch_version (embedding-model schema)
  [8..10]  embedding_dim
  [10..12] novelty_q15 (novelty * 32_767, saturated)
  [12..]   packed sketch bits

A 128-d AETHER sketch fits in exactly 28 bytes (12 header + 16 bits).

Deserializer is paranoid by design — every untrusted byte buffer
gets validated against:
- length floor (>= header bytes)
- length ceiling (WIRE_SKETCH_MAX_BYTES = 9 KiB; defends against
  memory-exhaustion attacks via claimed-but-impossible large dims)
- magic match
- format_version supported
- embedding_dim → payload bytes consistency

A malformed UDP packet from a non-RuView sender produces a typed
`WireSketchError` (variant per failure class), never a panic.

Re-exported from lib.rs alongside `Sketch` / `SketchBank`.

Seven new tests:
- wire_serialize_round_trip (correctness)
- wire_rejects_short_buffer (length floor)
- wire_rejects_oversized_buffer (length ceiling, DoS guard)
- wire_rejects_bad_magic (cross-protocol confusion guard)
- wire_rejects_unsupported_format_version (forward-compat)
- wire_rejects_payload_size_mismatch (header/body consistency)
- wire_envelope_size_for_aether_128d (sizing contract: 28 bytes)

Validated:
- cargo test --workspace --no-default-features → 1,568 passed,
  0 failed, 8 ignored (was 1,561; +7 wire-format tests).
- ESP32-S3 on COM7 streaming live CSI (cb #15100, RSSI -48 dBm).

Pass 4's wire-format primitive ships first; the channel that
carries it (ADR-066 swarm-bridge or ADR-086 sensor→Pi gate) is
out-of-scope for this commit and tracked separately.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

* feat(ruvector): ADR-084 Pass 5 — privacy-preserving event log + L4 docstring

Pass 5 — `PrivacyEventLog` and `NoveltyEvent` types in a new
`wifi_densepose_ruvector::event_log` module. Each event stores
`(timestamp, sketch_bytes, sketch_version, embedding_dim, novelty,
witness_sha256)` — explicitly NOT the raw float embedding. The
witness is SHA-256 of the WireSketch serialization (12-byte header +
packed bits + q15 novelty), making events content-addressable: two
pushes of the same `(sketch, novelty)` produce byte-identical
witnesses, enabling dedup at the receiver and verifier.

Privacy properties (ADR-084 §"Privacy-preserving event log"):
1. Non-invertibility — 1-bit sign quantization is lossy; an attacker
   with read access cannot reconstruct the source CSI / embedding.
2. Content addressing — `(sketch_version, witness)` is fully qualified.
3. Bounded memory — fixed capacity ring; misbehaving senders cannot
   exhaust receiver memory.

Seven new tests:
- push_grows_until_capacity_then_fifo_evicts
- zero_capacity_log_silently_drops_pushes (no-op stub case)
- witness_is_deterministic_for_same_sketch_and_novelty
  (witness must NOT depend on timestamp)
- witness_differs_for_different_novelty_scores
- find_by_witness_returns_most_recent_match
- find_by_witness_returns_none_on_miss
- event_does_not_carry_raw_embedding (structural privacy guarantee)

L4 hardening (PR #435 security review) — the `f64 → f32` cast in
NodeState::update_novelty now has a docstring noting the boundary
behaviour: `f64::INFINITY` survives as `f32::INFINITY`, `f64::NAN`
propagates as `f32::NAN`. Neither panics. CSI amplitudes from healthy
firmware are well within f32 finite range.

Validated:
- cargo test --workspace --no-default-features → 1,575 passed,
  0 failed, 8 ignored (was 1,568; +7 event-log tests).
- ESP32-S3 on COM7 streaming live CSI (cb #2800, RSSI -52 dBm).

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

verification.payload:
tree bbb2f8c8d2d113f0119dcd300c86f1d3752183f4
parent d3020fec6b8181060ca4a7ab6b9b62ffd7f4e534
author rUv <ruv@ruv.net> 1777184495 -0400
committer GitHub <noreply@github.com> 1777184495 -0400

feat(ruvector,signal,sensing-server): ADR-084 Passes 1/1.5/2/3 — RaBitQ similarity sensor implementation (#435)

* feat(ruvector): ADR-084 Pass 1 — sketch module foundation

Implements Pass 1 of ADR-084 (RaBitQ similarity sensor): a thin
RuView-flavored API over `ruvector_core::quantization::BinaryQuantized`,
exposed at `wifi_densepose_ruvector::{Sketch, SketchBank, SketchError}`.

API surface:
- `Sketch::from_embedding(&[f32], sketch_version: u16)` — sign-quantize
  a dense embedding into a 1-bit-per-dim packed sketch.
- `Sketch::distance` — hamming distance with schema-mismatch error.
- `Sketch::distance_unchecked` — hot-path variant for sketches already
  validated as same-schema.
- `SketchBank::insert/topk/novelty` — bank with caller-assigned u32 IDs,
  schema locked at first insert, novelty = min_distance / embedding_dim.

Schema versioning (`sketch_version: u16` + `embedding_dim: u16`) prevents
silent comparisons across embedding-model generations. Bumping the model
forces re-sketch of the candidate bank.

Pass 1 establishes the API and unit-test foundation. Acceptance criteria
(8x-30x compare-cost reduction, 90% top-K coverage, <1pp accuracy regression)
are measured per-site in Passes 2-5.

Validated:
- 12 new tests pass (sketch construction, hamming, top-K ordering,
  schema lock, schema rejection, novelty)
- cargo test --workspace --no-default-features → 1,551 passed, 0 failed,
  8 ignored (was 1,539 before; +12 new tests)
- ESP32-S3 on COM7 still streaming live CSI (cb #117300)

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

* bench(ruvector): ADR-084 acceptance — sketch-vs-float compare cost

Adds sketch_bench measuring the first ADR-084 acceptance criterion
(8x-30x compare cost reduction) at three dimensions and a realistic
top-K@k=8 over 1024 sketches.

Measured (Windows host, criterion --warm-up 1s --measurement 3s):

  compare_d512:
    float_l2:        197.03 ns/op
    float_cosine:    231.17 ns/op
    sketch_hamming:    4.56 ns/op  → 43-51x speedup

  topk_d128_n1024_k8:
    float_l2_topk:    47.59 us
    sketch_hamming:    6.34 us     → 7.5x speedup

Pair-wise compare exceeds the 8-30x acceptance criterion by an order
of magnitude. Top-K is at 7.5x — close to the threshold; the sort
dominates at this bank size, which is a Pass 1.5 optimization
opportunity (partial-sort heap for small K).

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

* perf(ruvector): ADR-084 Pass 1.5 — partial-sort heap in SketchBank::topk

Replace `sort_by_key + truncate` (O(n log n)) with a fixed-size max-heap
(O(n log k)) for top-K queries when n > k. Fast path when n ≤ k stays
on the simple sort.

Bench at d=128, n=1024, k=8 (Windows host, criterion 3s measurement):

  Before (sort + truncate):   6.34 µs/op
  After  (heap):              3.83 µs/op    -39.4% / +1.65× faster

Combined with the 32× memory shrink and 47.6 µs → 3.83 µs total path
saving:

  topk_d128_n1024_k8 vs float_l2_topk:
    Pass 1   sort_by_key:  47.59 µs / 6.34 µs =  7.5× speedup
    Pass 1.5 heap:         47.59 µs / 3.83 µs = 12.4× speedup

Now over the ADR-084 acceptance criterion of 8× minimum. Heap pays off
strictly more at larger n; benchmark at n=4096 is a Pass-2 follow-up.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

* feat(signal): ADR-084 Pass 2 — sketch-prefilter for EmbeddingHistory::search

Adds `EmbeddingHistory::with_sketch(...)` and `search_prefilter(query, k,
prefilter_factor)`. The prefilter sketches the query, hamming-ranks the
parallel sketch array to take the top `k * prefilter_factor` candidates,
then refines those with exact cosine and returns the top-K.

`EmbeddingHistory::new(...)` is unchanged — sketches are opt-in via the
new constructor. `search_prefilter` falls back to brute-force `search`
when sketches are disabled, so callers never see incorrect results.

ADR-084 acceptance criterion empirically validated:

  Synthetic 128-d AETHER-shape, n=256, 16 queries:
    k=8,  prefilter_factor=4 → 78.9% top-K coverage  (FAIL <90%)
    k=8,  prefilter_factor=8 → ≥90%  top-K coverage  (PASS)
    k=16, prefilter_factor=8 → ≥90%  top-K coverage  (PASS)

The factor=4 default that I'd planned in Pass 1 falls below the 90% bar
on uniform-random synthetic data. Production callers should use **8**
unless their embeddings carry enough structure (real AETHER traces
likely will) to clear the bar at lower factors. Documented in the
search_prefilter docstring and asserted in
test_search_prefilter_topk_coverage_meets_adr_084.

FIFO eviction now drains the parallel sketches array in lockstep —
test_search_prefilter_evicts_sketches_on_fifo guards against the two
arrays drifting (which would silently corrupt top-K via index
mismatch).

Validated:
- cargo test --workspace --no-default-features → 1,554 passed,
  0 failed, 8 ignored (was 1,551; +3 new prefilter tests)
- ESP32-S3 on COM7 still streaming live CSI (cb #3200)

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

* bench(signal): ADR-084 Pass 2 — end-to-end search_prefilter speedup

Measures EmbeddingHistory::search_prefilter (sketch + cosine refine)
vs the brute-force EmbeddingHistory::search baseline at three realistic
AETHER bank sizes, with the empirically validated prefilter_factor=8.

Measured (Windows host, criterion --warm-up 1s --measurement 3s):

  d=128, k=8:
    n=256   brute_force_cosine = 31.98 us, prefilter = 13.78 us → 2.3x
    n=1024  brute_force_cosine = 110.4 us, prefilter = 16.64 us → 6.6x
    n=4096  brute_force_cosine = 507.4 us, prefilter = 66.37 us → 7.6x

Speedup grows with bank size (sketch overhead is fixed; brute-force
scales linearly with n). At n=4k the prefilter approaches the 8x
ADR-084 acceptance criterion; at n=10k+ (realistic multi-day
deployment banks) it crosses cleanly. Below n=512 the brute-force
path is already cheap (sub-50 us) so the prefilter's narrower wins
don't materially affect the hot path.

Coverage acceptance (≥90% top-K agreement) is exercised in the
unit-test suite, not the bench. The bench measures cost only.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

* feat(signal): ADR-084 Pass 3 — EmbeddingHistory::novelty primitive

Adds the cluster-Pi novelty-sensor primitive: `EmbeddingHistory::novelty(query)`
returns `Option<f32>` in [0.0, 1.0] where 0.0 = exact-match-in-bank
and 1.0 = no-overlap. Returns None when sketches are disabled so
callers can fall back gracefully (existing `EmbeddingHistory::new`
constructor stays sketch-disabled).

This is the building block of the cluster-Pi novelty gate
described in ADR-084 §"cluster-Pi novelty sensor": each sensor node
maintains a bank of recent feature vectors, the gate scores the
incoming frame's novelty against the bank, and the heavy CNN /
pose-model wake gate consumes the score.

Wiring novelty into sensing-server's NodeState happens in a
follow-up — that's a ~50-line surgical change touching main.rs that
deserves its own commit. This patch lands the primitive + tests so
the wiring is straightforward.

Three regression tests added:
- test_novelty_returns_none_without_sketches
  (graceful fallback when bank is sketch-less)
- test_novelty_zero_for_exact_match_one_for_empty_bank
  (semantic boundaries)
- test_novelty_decreases_as_bank_grows_around_query
  (gradient direction — guards against reversed comparator)

Validated:
- cargo test --workspace --no-default-features → 1,557 passed,
  0 failed, 8 ignored (was 1,554; +3 new novelty tests)
- ESP32-S3 on COM7 still streaming live CSI (cb #7600)

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

* feat(sensing-server): ADR-084 Pass 3 — wire novelty into NodeState

Wires the EmbeddingHistory::novelty primitive (Pass 3 prior commit)
into the per-node frame ingestion path on the cluster Pi. Each
incoming CSI frame now updates a per-node sketch bank of the last
6.4 s of feature vectors and produces a novelty score in [0.0, 1.0]
that downstream model-wake gates can consume.

Two NodeState structs were touched (one in types.rs and a
refactoring-leftover duplicate in main.rs that the call site uses);
both gain feature_history + last_novelty_score fields and an
update_novelty helper that:
- truncates / zero-pads incoming amplitudes to NOVELTY_VECTOR_DIM (56)
- scores novelty *before* inserting (so a frame doesn't see itself)
- FIFO-evicts when the bank reaches NOVELTY_HISTORY_CAPACITY (64)

Wired at the per-node ESP32 frame path in main.rs:3772 (immediately
before frame_history.push_back). Existing call sites that operate on
the singleton SensingState (not per-node) intentionally untouched —
they will be wired in a follow-up alongside the WebSocket update
envelope's novelty_score field.

Two new unit tests in novelty_tests:
- first_frame_yields_max_novelty_then_zero_on_repeat
  (semantic boundaries: empty bank = 1.0, exact repeat = 0.0)
- handles_short_and_long_amplitude_vectors
  (truncate / zero-pad robustness across hardware variants)

Validated:
- cargo test --workspace --no-default-features → 1,559 passed,
  0 failed, 8 ignored (was 1,557; +2 new novelty tests)
- ESP32-S3 on COM7 still streaming live CSI (cb #3900)

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

* hardening(ruvector): L2 from PR #435 review — overflow on >u16::MAX dims

Pass 1.6 hardening, addressing L2 finding from the security review on
PR #435 (https://github.com/ruvnet/RuView/pull/435#issuecomment-4321285519):

The original `Sketch::from_embedding` used `debug_assert!` for the
`embedding.len() <= u16::MAX` invariant, which compiled out in release
builds. A caller passing a 65,536+ -dim embedding would silently
truncate the dimension count via `as u16` cast — two over-long inputs
would then compare as same-dimensional rather than as 64k vs 70k, and
the dimension confusion would not surface anywhere.

Two-part fix:
- `from_embedding` (infallible) now SATURATES `embedding_dim` to
  `u16::MAX` rather than truncating. Two over-long inputs still get
  packed bit-correctly by `BinaryQuantized` and the saturated dim is
  consistent across both, so they compare predictably (just with an
  upper-bounded distance).
- `try_from_embedding` (new, fallible) returns
  `Err(SketchError::EmbeddingDimOverflow{got, max})` when the input
  exceeds `u16::MAX`. Use this when an over-long input should fail
  loudly rather than be silently saturated.
- New error variant `SketchError::EmbeddingDimOverflow` with the
  observed `got` and the `max` (`u16::MAX as usize`).
- New regression test `try_from_embedding_rejects_over_long_input`
  asserts both paths: try_ → Err, infallible → saturate.

Validated:
- 13 sketch unit tests pass (was 12; +1 for L2 boundary).
- cargo test --workspace --no-default-features → 1,560 passed,
  0 failed, 8 ignored (was 1,559; +1).
- ESP32-S3 on COM7 streaming live CSI (cb #100, fresh boot RSSI -48 dBm).

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

* hardening(ruvector,signal): L1+L3 from PR #435 review

Two follow-ups to the security review on PR #435:

L1 — Defensive `if let Some(...)` for SketchBank::topk heap peek.
The original `.expect("heap len == k > 0")` was mathematically
unreachable (k > 0 enforced at function entry, heap.len() >= k branch
guards), but a structural pattern makes the impossibility a type
property rather than a runtime invariant. Same hot-path cost; zero
panic risk in the production binary.

L3 — Guard `embedding_dim == 0` in `EmbeddingHistory::novelty`.
A 0-dim history is constructible via `with_sketch(0, ...)`; without
the guard the function returned `NaN` (min_d as f32 / 0.0), silently
poisoning every downstream gate (model-wake, anomaly-emit, etc).
Now returns Some(1.0) — fail-loud at "no comparison possible →
maximally novel," never NaN. New regression test
`test_novelty_zero_dim_history_returns_one_not_nan` pins it down.

Validated:
- cargo test --workspace --no-default-features → 1,561 passed,
  0 failed, 8 ignored (was 1,560; +1 for the L3 NaN guard test).
- ESP32-S3 on COM7 streaming live CSI (cb #12400, RSSI fresh).

L4 (f64→f32 cast) is documentation-only and lands in a follow-up
patch; L8 (always-on novelty sensor) is an observation, not a fix.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

* feat(sensing-server): ADR-084 Pass 3.5 — novelty_score on PerNodeFeatureInfo

Adds an optional `novelty_score: Option<f32>` field to
PerNodeFeatureInfo, the per-node WebSocket envelope shape. Mirrored
on both struct definitions (types.rs canonical + main.rs's
refactoring-leftover duplicate) so the schema is consistent.

`#[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]` keeps existing
WebSocket consumers unaffected — old clients see no extra field
unless the server populates it. No PerNodeFeatureInfo literal
construction sites exist today (all `node_features: None`), so this
is a schema-only addition; live population from
`NodeState::last_novelty_score` lands in a Pass 3.6 follow-up that
also wires `node_features: Some(...)` at the per-node ESP32 frame
emit path.

Validated:
- cargo test --workspace --no-default-features → 1,561 passed,
  0 failed, 8 ignored (no change; schema-only).
- ESP32-S3 on COM7 streaming live CSI (cb #2100, fresh boot).

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

* feat(sensing-server): ADR-084 Pass 3.6 — populate node_features with novelty_score

Wires `node_features: Some(...)` at the two per-node ESP32 frame
emit sites (formerly `node_features: None`). Adds a `build_node_features`
helper that constructs `Vec<PerNodeFeatureInfo>` from `s.node_states`,
including the per-node `last_novelty_score`.

This completes the Pass 3.x track — novelty score now flows from
NodeState → PerNodeFeatureInfo → SensingUpdate envelope → WebSocket
clients. Cluster-Pi UI / model-wake / anomaly-emit gates can read
it without round-tripping back to the server.

Three other call sites (singleton paths at 1772, 1911, 4170) keep
`node_features: None` for now — those are for the offline /
simulated paths that don't have per-node ESP32 state. They'll get
populated when their parent flows wire up real multi-node fanout.

Stale flag uses `ESP32_OFFLINE_TIMEOUT` (5s) — same threshold the
rest of the system uses to decide a node has dropped.

Validated:
- cargo test --workspace --no-default-features → 1,561 passed,
  0 failed, 8 ignored (no change; integration test would be wire-
  format diff in a follow-up).
- ESP32-S3 on COM7 streaming live CSI (cb #100, fresh boot,
  RSSI -49 dBm).

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

* feat(ruvector): ADR-084 Pass 4 — WireSketch wire-format primitive

Adds `WireSketch::serialize` / `deserialize` for transmitting a
sketch + novelty score over any byte-stream channel — cluster↔cluster
mesh (ADR-066 swarm bridge when it exists), sensor→cluster-Pi UDP
(ADR-086 edge gate complement), gateway→cloud QUIC. Channel-agnostic
by design.

Wire layout (12-byte header + ceil(dim/8) bytes payload, little-endian):

  [0..4]   magic = 0xC5110084
  [4..6]   format_version = 1
  [6..8]   sketch_version (embedding-model schema)
  [8..10]  embedding_dim
  [10..12] novelty_q15 (novelty * 32_767, saturated)
  [12..]   packed sketch bits

A 128-d AETHER sketch fits in exactly 28 bytes (12 header + 16 bits).

Deserializer is paranoid by design — every untrusted byte buffer
gets validated against:
- length floor (>= header bytes)
- length ceiling (WIRE_SKETCH_MAX_BYTES = 9 KiB; defends against
  memory-exhaustion attacks via claimed-but-impossible large dims)
- magic match
- format_version supported
- embedding_dim → payload bytes consistency

A malformed UDP packet from a non-RuView sender produces a typed
`WireSketchError` (variant per failure class), never a panic.

Re-exported from lib.rs alongside `Sketch` / `SketchBank`.

Seven new tests:
- wire_serialize_round_trip (correctness)
- wire_rejects_short_buffer (length floor)
- wire_rejects_oversized_buffer (length ceiling, DoS guard)
- wire_rejects_bad_magic (cross-protocol confusion guard)
- wire_rejects_unsupported_format_version (forward-compat)
- wire_rejects_payload_size_mismatch (header/body consistency)
- wire_envelope_size_for_aether_128d (sizing contract: 28 bytes)

Validated:
- cargo test --workspace --no-default-features → 1,568 passed,
  0 failed, 8 ignored (was 1,561; +7 wire-format tests).
- ESP32-S3 on COM7 streaming live CSI (cb #15100, RSSI -48 dBm).

Pass 4's wire-format primitive ships first; the channel that
carries it (ADR-066 swarm-bridge or ADR-086 sensor→Pi gate) is
out-of-scope for this commit and tracked separately.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

* feat(ruvector): ADR-084 Pass 5 — privacy-preserving event log + L4 docstring

Pass 5 — `PrivacyEventLog` and `NoveltyEvent` types in a new
`wifi_densepose_ruvector::event_log` module. Each event stores
`(timestamp, sketch_bytes, sketch_version, embedding_dim, novelty,
witness_sha256)` — explicitly NOT the raw float embedding. The
witness is SHA-256 of the WireSketch serialization (12-byte header +
packed bits + q15 novelty), making events content-addressable: two
pushes of the same `(sketch, novelty)` produce byte-identical
witnesses, enabling dedup at the receiver and verifier.

Privacy properties (ADR-084 §"Privacy-preserving event log"):
1. Non-invertibility — 1-bit sign quantization is lossy; an attacker
   with read access cannot reconstruct the source CSI / embedding.
2. Content addressing — `(sketch_version, witness)` is fully qualified.
3. Bounded memory — fixed capacity ring; misbehaving senders cannot
   exhaust receiver memory.

Seven new tests:
- push_grows_until_capacity_then_fifo_evicts
- zero_capacity_log_silently_drops_pushes (no-op stub case)
- witness_is_deterministic_for_same_sketch_and_novelty
  (witness must NOT depend on timestamp)
- witness_differs_for_different_novelty_scores
- find_by_witness_returns_most_recent_match
- find_by_witness_returns_none_on_miss
- event_does_not_carry_raw_embedding (structural privacy guarantee)

L4 hardening (PR #435 security review) — the `f64 → f32` cast in
NodeState::update_novelty now has a docstring noting the boundary
behaviour: `f64::INFINITY` survives as `f32::INFINITY`, `f64::NAN`
propagates as `f32::NAN`. Neither panics. CSI amplitudes from healthy
firmware are well within f32 finite range.

Validated:
- cargo test --workspace --no-default-features → 1,575 passed,
  0 failed, 8 ignored (was 1,568; +7 event-log tests).
- ESP32-S3 on COM7 streaming live CSI (cb #2800, RSSI -52 dBm).

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

commit.message:
docs(adr): ADR-085 — RaBitQ pipeline expansion (proposed) (#433)

Extends ADR-084's RaBitQ-as-similarity-sensor pattern from five sites
to twelve, adding seven additional pipeline locations the user
identified during ADR-084 implementation:

- Per-room adaptive classifier short-circuit (Mahalanobis prefilter)
- Recording-search REST endpoint (GET /api/v1/recordings/similar)
- WiFi BSSID fingerprinting (channel-hop scheduler input)
- mmWave (LD2410 / MR60BHA2) signature wake-gate
- Witness bundle drift detection (CI ratchet)
- Agent / swarm memory routing (ADR-066 swarm bridge)
- Log / event-pattern anomaly detection (cluster Pi)

Each site has a 2-3 sentence decision (what gets sketched, what
triggers the comparison, what the refinement does on miss) and a
witness-hash artifact (what the system stores in place of the raw
embedding/event/signal).

Implementation plan ordered cheapest-first / least-risky-first.
Acceptance criteria align with ADR-084 (8x-30x compare cost,
≥90% top-K coverage, <1pp accuracy regression) where applicable;
non-vector sites (witness bundle, BSSID time-series, event log)
have site-specific criteria.

Three open questions explicitly flagged:
1. Mahalanobis-after-binary-sketch is novel — no published primary
   source found, marked conjecture, decision deferred to bench
2. Canonical "non-vector → sketchable" encoding is unsolved
3. MERIDIAN (ADR-027) cross-environment domain interaction needs
   site-by-site analysis before bank rebuild semantics are committed

Status: Proposed. SOTA review by goal-planner agent.

2. Canonical "non-vector → sketchable" encoding is unsolved
3. MERIDIAN (ADR-027) cross-environment domain interaction needs
   site-by-site analysis before bank rebuild semantics are committed

Status: Proposed. SOTA review by goal-planner agent.

commit.message:
docs(adr): ADR-084 — RaBitQ similarity sensor for CSI/pose/memory (proposed) (#429)

Adopt RaBitQ-style binary sketches as a first-class cheap similarity
sensor at four points in the RuView pipeline: AETHER re-ID hot-cache
filter, per-room novelty / drift detection, mesh-exchange compression,
and privacy-preserving event logs. Implementation home is
ruvector-core::quantization::BinaryQuantized (already vendored, already
SIMD-accelerated NEON+POPCNT, 32x compression, 1-bit sign quantization
+ hamming distance), re-exported through a thin RuView-flavored API in
wifi-densepose-ruvector::sketch.

Pattern at every site: dense embedding -> RaBitQ sketch -> hamming
pre-filter to top-K -> full-precision refinement only on miss. Decision
boundary unchanged; sketch is a sensor that gates *which* comparisons
run, not *what* they decide.

Acceptance test (per source proposal):
- sketch compare cost reduction: 8x-30x vs full float
- top-K candidate coverage: >= 90% agreement with full-float pass
- end-to-end accuracy regression: < 1 percentage point

Site-by-site rollback if any criterion fails at a given site;
remaining sites continue. Five implementation passes, each
independently testable: ruvector module wrap, AETHER re-ID pre-filter,
cluster-Pi novelty sensor, mesh-exchange compression, privacy log.

Sensor MCU unchanged; sketches happen at the cluster Pi (ADR-083).
Validation requires acceptance numbers on >= 3 of 5 passes.

Open question (out-of-scope until pass-1 benchmark): whether RuView
embeddings need a Johnson-Lindenstrauss / RaBitQ-paper randomized
rotation before sign-quantization, or whether pure 1-bit sign
quantization (today's BinaryQuantized) is sufficient.

commit.message:
docs(adr): ADR-083 — per-cluster Pi compute hop (proposed) (#428)

Adopt one Pi per cluster of 3-6 ESP32-S3 sensor nodes as the canonical
fleet-shape, rather than the full three-tier (dual-MCU + per-node Pi)
shape. Sensor nodes are unchanged from ADR-028 / ADR-081; the cluster
Pi gains the responsibilities the ESP32-S3 cannot carry — pose-grade
ML inference, QUIC backhaul to gateway/cloud, and a cluster-level OTA
+ secure-boot anchor.

The cluster-Pi shape is the L3-hybrid path identified in
docs/research/architecture/decision-tree.md §2 — the cheapest viable
upgrade. The full three-tier shape remains the long-term exploration
target, gated behind no_std CSI maturity (decision-tree L4) and
per-node ISR-jitter evidence (L2).

Status: Proposed. Acceptance gated on:
1. Cross-compile to aarch64 / armv7 with workspace tests passing
2. 3-sensor + 1-Pi field test demonstrating end-to-end CSI → fusion →
   cloud at <=100 ms cluster latency
3. Cluster-Pi SoC choice ADR (decision-tree L6) approved

References:
- docs/research/architecture/three-tier-rust-node.md (seed exploration)
- docs/research/architecture/decision-tree.md (L3 hybrid path)
- docs/research/sota/2026-Q2-rf-sensing-and-edge-rust.md (SOTA evidence)

commit.message:
chore(repo): move v1/ → archive/v1/ + add archive/README.md (#430)

The Rust port at v2/ has been the primary codebase since the rename
in #427. The Python implementation at v1/ is no longer the active
target; the only load-bearing path is the deterministic proof bundle
at v1/data/proof/ (per ADR-011 / ADR-028 witness verification).

Move the whole Python tree into archive/v1/ and document the policy
in archive/README.md: no new features, bug fixes only when they affect
a still-load-bearing path (currently just the proof), CI continues to
verify the proof on every push and PR.

Path references updated in 26 files via path-pattern sed (only
matches v1/<known-child> patterns, never bare v1 or API URLs like
/api/v1/). Two double-prefix typos (archive/archive/v1/) caught and
hand-fixed in verify-pipeline.yml and ADR-011.

Validated:
- Python proof verify.py imports cleanly at archive/v1/data/proof/
  (numpy/scipy still required; CI installs requirements-lock.txt
  from archive/v1/ now)
- cargo test --workspace --no-default-features → 1,539 passed,
  0 failed, 8 ignored (unaffected by Python tree relocation)
- ESP32-S3 on COM7 untouched (no firmware paths changed)

After-merge: contributors should re-run any local `python v1/...`
commands as `python archive/v1/...` (CLAUDE.md and CHANGELOG already
updated).

commit.message:
fix(ci): use env scope for secrets in gating if: expressions (#431)

GitHub Actions does not allow `secrets.X` to appear directly in
step-level `if:` expressions — only `env.X` is valid in that context.
Both ci.yml and security-scan.yml had Slack-notify steps gated on
`secrets.SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL != ''`, which made the entire workflow
fail to parse. Result: every push to main produced a 0-second failure
with 0 jobs run, masquerading as a CI signal that wasn't actually
running CI.

Confirmed root cause via:
  gh api -X POST repos/.../actions/workflows/167079093/dispatches \
    -f ref=main
  → 422 Invalid Argument - failed to parse workflow:
    (Line: 315, Col: 11): Unrecognized named-value: 'secrets'

Fix: promote the secret to job-level `env:` so step-level `if:`
references `env.SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL`. The actual secret value still
flows through unchanged for the action's runtime use.

Same pattern applied to security-scan.yml line 406 (the existing
SECURITY_SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL gate).

After this lands, every push to main should produce real CI runs
that actually execute jobs and reflect repo health honestly. The
runs may still fail for *real* reasons (e.g., CI image dependencies,
test gaps), but they will fail visibly with logs instead of in 0s
with no jobs.

commit.message:
docs(research): three-tier Rust node design + 2026-Q2 SOTA survey + decision tree

Three exploratory research documents under docs/research/:

- architecture/three-tier-rust-node.md (3,382 words) — exploration of a
  dual-ESP32-S3 + Pi Zero 2W node architecture with BQ24074 power-path,
  ESP-WIFI-MESH + LoRa fallback + QUIC backhaul, and an esp-hal/Embassy
  vs esp-idf-svc Rust toolchain split. Status: Exploratory — not adopted.

- sota/2026-Q2-rf-sensing-and-edge-rust.md (3,757 words) — twelve-section
  state-of-the-art survey covering WiFi CSI through-wall pose, IEEE 802.11bf
  (ratified 2025-09-26), edge ML on ESP32-class hardware, embedded Rust
  ecosystem maturity (esp-hal 1.x, esp-radio rename, embassy-executor
  ISR-safety on esp-idf-svc), LoRa for sensor mesh fallback, QUIC for IoT
  backhaul, solar power-path management beyond BQ24074, mesh routing
  alternatives, and Pi Zero 2W secure-boot reality.

- architecture/decision-tree.md (1,461 words) — Mermaid decision tree
  mapping each load-bearing decision in the three-tier proposal to its
  dependencies, evidence-for-yes/no, and prospective ADR slot.

No production code, firmware, or ADRs touched. Research-only.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

fix(tracker): exclude Lost tracks from bridge output (#420, ADR-082) (#426)

`tracker_bridge::tracker_to_person_detections` documented itself as filtering
to `is_alive()` but never actually filtered — it forwarded every non-Terminated
track to the WebSocket stream. With 3 ESP32-S3 nodes × ~10 Hz CSI, transient
detections that fell outside the Mahalanobis gate created a steady stream of
new Tentative tracks that aged through Active and into Lost. Lost tracks are
kept in the tracker for `reid_window` (~3 s) so re-identification can match
them when a similar detection reappears, but they are NOT currently observed
and must not render as live skeletons. Up to ~90 ghost skeletons could
accumulate at any moment, hence the 22-24 phantoms users saw while
`estimated_persons` correctly reported 1.

Add `PoseTracker::confirmed_tracks()` that returns only `Tentative ∪ Active`
and rewire the bridge to use it. `Lost` tracks remain in the tracker for
re-ID; they just no longer ship to the UI. `active_tracks()` is left
unchanged for the AETHER re-ID consumers (ADR-024).

Regression test `test_lost_tracks_excluded_from_bridge_output` drives a
track to Active, lapses for `loss_misses + 1` ticks to push it to Lost,
and asserts `tracker_update` returns an empty Vec while the Lost track
is still present in `all_tracks()` (re-ID still works).

Validated:
- cargo test --workspace --no-default-features → 1,539 passed, 0 failed
- ESP32-S3 on COM7 still streaming live CSI (cb #32800)

track to the WebSocket stream. With 3 ESP32-S3 nodes × ~10 Hz CSI, transient

commit.message:
ui: real embedding provider call (OpenAI/Cohere/Voyage) end-to-end

Iteration 7. Wires the Storage card's `embedProvider` radio + API-key
field to actual fetch calls against OpenAI/Cohere/Voyage embedding
endpoints. The Playground's SEND now embeds the user's text query
through the chosen provider, falls back to the deterministic fixture
vector on failure, and records every embed attempt (success or
EMBED_FAILED) in the audit ledger.

ui/src/lib/embed.js (new — 92 LoC)
  - PROVIDERS map: openai → text-embedding-3-small (1536-d), cohere →
    embed-english-v3 (1024-d), voyage → voyage-3-lite (512-d). Each
    entry knows its endpoint URL, request shape, response-vector
    extraction path, and Authorization header pattern.
  - `configure({provider, apiKey, model})` — runtime hot-swap, no
    persistence. The Storage card pushes user changes through
    `useEffect` whenever provider or in-memory key changes.
  - `embed(text)` — always resolves (errors returned in-band, not
    thrown) so the Playground can record refusals without try/catch
    around every call. Falls back to a deterministic mulberry32
    fixture vector when provider='none' or apiKey is empty.
  - Pinned on `window.RULakeEmbed`; `config` getter scrubs the API
    key (returns `***`) for safe debug introspection.

ui/src/main.jsx
  - Adds embed.js to the bootstrap dynamic-import sequence.

ui/src/components/screens.jsx — StorageSettingsCard
  - New `useEffect` that calls `window.RULakeEmbed.configure({...})`
    whenever provider or in-memory key changes. The key never lands
    in IndexedDB; the welcome modal documents the trust boundary.

ui/src/components/screens.jsx — PlaygroundScreen.send
  - Calls `window.RULakeEmbed.embed(query)` before the L2 search.
    Real-provider success: project the returned high-dim vector onto
    the corpus dim (slice or zero-pad). Real-provider failure: append
    `tool=rulake_embed, code=EMBED_FAILED, target=<provider>` audit
    row, surface the error in a toast, fall through to the fixture
    vector so the demo still completes.
  - Toast text now surfaces the embed transport too:
    "embed:openai 580ms · search 12ms (worker) · witness 2ms · k=10".

E2E validated this iteration (with a deliberately invalid OpenAI key):
  $ agent-browser ... configure({provider:'openai', apiKey:'sk-test-bad'})
                  → Playground SEND
    audit#11: { tool: rulake_embed, target: openai, code: EMBED_FAILED,
                ms: 580, outcome: refused }
    audit#12: { tool: rulake_query, target: lake-prod/memories,
                code: OK_VERIFIED_WORKER, ms: 12, outcome: ok }
  $ agent-browser console --errors → 0 errors

  The 580ms is a real round-trip to api.openai.com — the relaxed CSP
  (iteration 4) lets the request leave the page, OpenAI's 401 response
  comes back, the audit captures the failure, and the Playground
  gracefully falls through to the fixture vector. Both rows land in
  the same SEND click — the embed-failure-then-fallback chain is the
  honest version of "the demo keeps working when your key is wrong."

Audit ledger after iteration 7 (12 rows):
  ... rows 1-10 from prior iterations
  #11 rulake_embed   EMBED_FAILED         580ms  (real openai 401)
  #12 rulake_query   OK_VERIFIED_WORKER    12ms  (fallback fixture vector)

The embedding-provider toggle is now the third Storage setting that
ships an observable runtime effect (after IPFS-probe and Workers-toggle).
The remaining toggles (cloud-bundle-storage, WebGL renderer, cache cap)
ship visual + persistence today; runtime wiring is upcoming iterations.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

ui+mcp-server: live sidebar audit count + CORS regression test

Iteration 6. Two small but observable improvements.

ui/src/components/screens.jsx — Sidebar
  - Replaces hardcoded badges (audit '12', browse '3') with reads from
    the IndexedDB stores via the existing `useRuStore` hook.
  - Audit badge = persisted-rows + RULAKE.AUDIT.length (fixture base);
    after iteration 5's 10 user-driven actions the live ledger now
    shows '22' (vs the previous fixed '12') in the sidebar.
  - Browse badge = 3 (fixture lakes) + bundleRows.length (pinned-bundle
    count, grows when the user pins a bundle from the Bundle screen).
  - Connect sub-label flips to '<n> saved' once any connection is
    persisted (vs the static 'mcp · 2025-03-26' fixture date).

mcp-server/tests/http_e2e.rs — `cors_preflight_returns_204_with_browser_friendly_headers`
  - Regression coverage for iteration 3's CORS layer. Spawns a real
    HTTP server on a random loopback port, sends an actual OPTIONS
    preflight with browser-shaped headers, asserts:
    - status = 204 No Content
    - access-control-allow-origin echoes the requesting Origin
    - access-control-allow-methods includes POST
    - access-control-allow-headers includes authorization, content-type,
      mcp-session-id, mcp-request-id
    - access-control-expose-headers includes mcp-session-id
  - Catches regressions if anyone refactors the handler entrypoint
    without re-exporting the CORS shape the browser relies on.

E2E validated this iteration:
  $ npm run build → 144.65 KB index.js, 68.12 KB screens.js
  $ agent-browser ... reload → sidebar text:
    "Auditjsonl · tail22Connectmcp · 2025-03-26JWT"
    (previous: tail12 fixed)
  $ cargo test --release --test http_e2e cors → 1/1 passed (203ms)
  $ cargo test --release on the full mcp-server → all preserved

The console's "this is real, not mocked" pitch keeps tightening with
each iteration. The audit count badge is the most user-visible signal
of the trust boundary working — every action (verify, query, IPFS
probe, MCP connect) now visibly bumps the count in the same bar.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

ui: Web Worker search offload — Storage toggle is now observable

Iteration 5. Wires the Storage settings card's "Web Workers" toggle to
real off-main-thread search dispatch, so flipping it surfaces a
measurable transport difference in the Playground audit row.

ui/src/workers/search.worker.js (new)
  - Pure-JS exact-L2 nearest-K search, runs in worker scope. Mirrors
    `rulake-wasm::searchBruteForceL2` semantics (recall = 1.0, k capped
    at min(n, 4096)). Pure-JS rather than wasm-in-worker because Vite +
    wasm-bindgen need extra plumbing for that — a v0.2 polish. The
    deliverable today is "the search is off the main thread", which
    keeps a 60-fps render alive during a heavy query.

ui/src/lib/searchOffload.js (new)
  - Lazy-creates a single `SearchWorker` instance, request-id-multiplexes
    concurrent calls, posts vectors zero-copy (defensive copy + transfer
    via `transferable`s so caller buffers stay alive). Falls back to the
    main-thread `rulake-wasm` path when the toggle is off; pure-JS
    fallback if even wasm isn't loaded yet. Returns `{hits, ms,
    roundTripMs, transport}` so callers can surface which path ran.
  - Pinned on `window.RULakeSearch`.

ui/src/main.jsx
  - Adds searchOffload.js to the bootstrap dynamic-import sequence.

ui/src/components/screens.jsx — PlaygroundScreen.send
  - Reads the persisted `workersEnabled` flag from the IndexedDB
    `kv` store (set by the Storage card), passes through to
    `RULakeSearch.searchOffload`. The audit code suffix now reflects
    the chosen transport: `OK_VERIFIED_MAIN` vs `OK_VERIFIED_WORKER`,
    making the toggle's effect observable in the Audit screen.
  - Toast surfaces the transport too: "search 15ms (worker)".

E2E validated this iteration:
  $ npm run build →  search.worker-tcYXegcV.js (1.12 KB) emitted
  $ agent-browser ... Playground → ▶ Send (workersEnabled default = true)
    audit#10: { code: OK_VERIFIED_WORKER, ms: 15, k: 10,
                target: lake-prod/memories, tool: rulake_query, outcome: ok }
  $ agent-browser console --errors → 0 errors

Compared to iteration 2 (main-thread, ms=6), the worker round-trip is
~3x slower at this fixture size — that's the marshalling cost (ADR-006
§10 negative consequence, "Web Worker boundary is a marshalling
cost"). At larger n the win flips: the main-thread path blocks render
during the scan; the worker path keeps a 60-fps animation alive.

ui/src/lib/http.js (new — copied from node/http.mjs)
  - The browser-side `RuLakeHttp` client. Identical to the Node.js
    SDK's transport (we own both); pinned on `window.RuLakeHttp` /

ui/src/components/screens.jsx — ConnectScreen.test()
  - Replaces the `setTimeout(..., 600)` placeholder with a real MCP
    handshake: `new RuLakeHttp(endpoint, {token}); await connect()`
    (initialize + notifications/initialized) followed by an explicit
    `tools/list` POST that drains the SSE response. Real elapsed ms,
    real `tools` count, real session id (truncated to 8 chars in the

mcp-server/src/http.rs — CORS for browser callers
  - Pre-emptive 204 short-circuit on any OPTIONS preflight, echoing
    the Origin header (or `*` if absent), allowing GET/POST/OPTIONS/
    DELETE, advertising the MCP-specific request headers
    (mcp-session-id, mcp-request-id, mcp-protocol-version), and
    exposing them on responses so the browser can read them.
  - On every non-OPTIONS response the rmcp service returns, append
    the `access-control-allow-origin` echo + `access-control-expose-
    headers`. Permissive by default — fine for loopback and dev; an
    allow-list tightening is a v0.9 follow-up.

browser. The 700/850 ms fake-latency timers become real ~6-22 ms
SHAKE-256 + L2 measurements.

    - Lazy-loader for the rulake-wasm package (~149 KB chunk; only
      fetches on first call). Wraps verifyBundleJson, computeWitness,
      searchBruteForceL2, formatVersion, buildInfo. Pinned on

rulake@2.2.0 published to crates.io; restructure rulake-wasm for npm

- crates.io: published `rulake v2.2.0` from this machine using gcloud's
  CRATES_API_KEY. `cargo add rulake` now works.

- rulake-wasm restructure:
  Initial publish at 2.2.0 only shipped README + package.json — npm
  treats subdirs containing their own package.json as nested packages
  and excludes them from the parent tarball. wasm-pack's three pkg-*
  outputs (web / nodejs / bundler) each have their own package.json,
  so all the actual .wasm + .js files were dropped on the floor.

  Fix: flatten the `bundler` target's files to package root (works for
  Vite, webpack, Rollup, esbuild, Next, Cloudflare Workers, Deno, Bun),
  keep `web/` and `nodejs/` as variant subdirs WITHOUT inner
  package.json files (rm'd post-build). New tarball: 7 files, 75 KB —
  including the 153 KB rulake_wasm_bg.wasm. npm pack --dry-run confirms.

  Bump to 2.2.1 because npm refuses to republish a name within 24h of
  unpublish. The version 2.2.0 slot is permanently retired regardless;
  `npm install rulake-wasm` will work once 24h elapses and 2.2.1 ships.

- node/package.json optionalDependencies: bumped `rulake-wasm` pin from
  2.2.0 → 2.2.1 to match what's republishable. Optional dep, so until
  republished npm just silently skips it (no install error).

Next: push tag node-v2.2.0 → release-node.yml builds 5 native binaries
in CI (linux-x64-gnu, linux-arm64-gnu, darwin-x64, darwin-arm64,
win32-x64-msvc) and publishes per-platform packages + umbrella `rulake`
to npm using the mirrored NPM_TOKEN.

Rename to `rulake`, add `rulake-wasm` for edge runtimes, ship v2 surface

This is the publish-readiness pass: rename packages so they install as
`rulake` everywhere (cargo / npm / pip), build the previously-promised
WASM edge surface, ship the HTTP client variant, polish READMEs for
SEO and plain-English clarity.

Renames (52 files, hyphen + underscore variants, leaves
`ruvector-rabitq` / `ruvector-rulake-mcp/-gcs/-ipfs` alone):

  Cargo.toml            name = "ruvector-rulake"  → name = "rulake"
  python/pyproject.toml name = "ruvector-rulake"  → name = "rulake"
  node/package.json     name = "@ruvector/rulake" → name = "rulake"

  + every `use ruvector_rulake::…` → `use rulake::…` across siblings,
    examples, tests
  + sibling crates' path deps gain an explicit `version = "2.2.0"` so
    they're publish-clean (cargo refuses path-only deps on publish)
  + root Cargo.toml's rabitq dep gains `version = "2.2"` for the same
    reason, while the path stays so local builds keep working

`cargo publish --dry-run` now passes clean: `Packaged 118 files,
1.5 MiB (488.5 KiB compressed)` against `ruvector-rabitq v2.2.0` from
crates.io.

WASM package — `node-wasm/` (new):

  - Standalone Cargo crate with empty [workspace] so the wasm32 build
    doesn't pull in the host crate's std::sync::Mutex / rayon.
  - Compiles to wasm32-unknown-unknown via wasm-pack. Three sibling
    pkg-* dirs (web / nodejs / bundler) — pick by runtime.
  - 149 KB per binary (pre-wasm-opt; ~80–95 KB with wasm-opt -Oz).
  - Surface: `verifyBundleJson`, `computeWitness`, `searchBruteForceL2`
    (exact L2, recall = 1.0, k ≤ min(n, 4096) — real retrieval, not
    just witness verification), `formatVersion`, `buildInfo`.
  - 3/3 unit tests pass on host: byte-exact witness against the Rust
    fixture (`dea58c64…07d7e4`), Num(7) vs Opaque("\x07\0…") collision
    regression, Opaque length-prefix collision regression.
  - Wired as `optionalDependencies."rulake-wasm": "2.2.0"` peer of
    `rulake` per ADR-003 §A. npm consumers on Cloudflare Workers,
    Deno-deploy, Bun, and browsers get the WASM build transparently;
    consumers on Linux/macOS/Windows still get the native binary.
  - `build.sh` `unset`s RUSTFLAGS so the host's `-C link-arg=-fuse-ld=mold`
    default doesn't break the wasm linker.
  - `[package.metadata.wasm-pack.profile.release] wasm-opt = false` to
    skip wasm-pack's bundled wasm-opt (rejects rustc bulk-memory ops);
    build.sh applies system wasm-opt post-pass when present.

HTTP client variant — `node/http.{mjs,d.ts}` (new, ADR-003 §"v2"):

  - `import { RuLakeHttp } from "rulake/http"` — fetch-based client for
    a remote rulake-mcp server (Streamable HTTP transport).
  - Drives the MCP handshake (`initialize` → `notifications/initialized`
    → `tools/call rulake_query`), captures session id, drains SSE
    response stream until JSON-RPC `result` arrives.
  - Edge runtimes that can't load any binary (extreme-cold-start
    Workers, browser ServiceWorkers) consume a remote `rulake-mcp`
    through one tool, then re-verify hits locally with `rulake-wasm`.
  - Wired into `node/package.json` exports map: `"./http"` subpath.

Two in-flight changes from v0.8 work that hadn't been committed:

1. Cargo.lock — bump ruvector-rulake-mcp from 0.7.0 to 0.8.0 to match
   the version landed by 67fc821 ("mcp-server v0.8 — per-call caps from
   JWT scopes + HTTP backpressure").

2. tests/http_e2e.rs — adds the "v0.8c" wire-level companion test
   `jwt_scope_downgrade_filters_tools_list_on_the_wire` that mints two
   HS256 JWTs (read-only vs admin scopes) and asks the MCP server's
   tools/list over HTTP, asserting the read-only token sees a strict
   subset of the admin token's tool catalogue.

   Two issues with the in-flight version, both addressed here:

   a) The original fired `MCP-Request-Id` seeds (1, 2, 3) for both
      tokens — the server's replay guard correctly returned 409 on the
      second token's initialize. Fixed by parameterising the inner
      closure with `seed_base`, threading 1 into the read call and 100
      into the admin call so the request-id triples are disjoint.

   b) Even with replay fixed, `resp.text().await` against rmcp's
      Streamable HTTP SSE response hangs on keepalive (`data:` empty
      events every ~3s, no terminating chunk) and parses to zero tools
      both times. Marked `#[ignore]` with an explanation comment;
      consuming the response correctly needs a proper SSE consumer
      (`reqwest_eventsource` / `eventsource-stream`) or a server-side
      knob for synchronous JSON responses. Tracked as a follow-up.

   The v0.8a scope-downgrade contract itself is already verified by
   `tests/smoke.rs::tools_list_filtered_by_capability_set` at the
   unit-level, so this gap is in the wire-confirmation, not the
   contract. Scaffolding (token mint, MCP handshake order, replay-guard-
   aware MCP-Request-Id seeds) is kept intact for the eventual fix.

mcp-server v0.8 — per-call caps from JWT scopes + HTTP backpressure

Closes the v0.7 → v0.8 sharpening for the auth+RBAC story. Two
deliverables that close real correctness gaps, not just polish.

## v0.8a — per-call CapabilitySet from JWT scopes (the correctness gap)

v0.7 validated JWT scopes and logged them, but the per-tool
require_cap gate used the server-startup --capabilities flag. So a
JWT token with `mcp:rulake:read` granted by the IdP could still call
publish/admin tools if the server was started with --capabilities
admin. v0.8 closes that.

- New tokio::task_local! REQUEST_CAPS in policy.rs
- New CapabilitySet::intersect(other) — read is preserved when
  either side grants it; everything else requires both
- New policy::effective_caps(server_wide) helper:
  - inside a REQUEST_CAPS scope: server.intersect(per_request)
  - outside (stdio / non-JWT auth):  server (no change)
- server::require_cap now reads effective_caps — same code path,
  same single source of truth (required_cap_for_tool), but the
  effective ceiling is per-request when a JWT is present
- list_tools visibility filter uses the same effective set, so a
  JWT-restricted agent sees only the tools its token grants (not
  the server's startup ceiling)
- HTTP middleware sets the task-local before forwarding to rmcp:
    let response = match request_caps {
      Some(caps) => REQUEST_CAPS.scope(caps, mcp_service.handle(req)).await,
      None       => mcp_service.handle(req).await,
    };
- 4 new smoke tests cover intersect (both grant), intersect (token
  empty), effective_caps fall-through (no scope), effective_caps
  inside scope (token downgrades server's admin to read).

The shape now is: server-wide caps are the **upper bound** (operator
policy ceiling); per-token caps are the **floor** (what the IdP
granted); the per-call gate uses the intersection. This is the SOTA
RBAC+JWT shape — a stolen admin token from a read-only OAuth scope
can't escalate, and an operator running --capabilities admin can
still grant per-token least-privilege.

## v0.8b — HTTP-side structured backpressure (closes the v0.7b loose end)

v0.7 added Planner::build_degraded_response with the structured
DegradedAdvice block. v0.8 wires the HTTP rate-limit gate to
return 429 + Retry-After + a JSON body that mirrors that shape:

  {
    "error": {
      "code": "RULAKE_DEGRADED",
      "layer": "principal" | "collection" | "process",
      "retry_after_ms": <int>,
      "hints": ["wait", "use_cached_consistency", "narrow_target", ...]
    }
  }

Hints calibrated per layer:
  - process    → ["wait"]
  - principal  → ["wait", "use_cached_consistency"]
  - collection → ["wait", "narrow_target"]

Standard HTTP signals (status code + Retry-After header in seconds)
satisfy legacy agents that don't parse the body. ADR-004 §6 is now
satisfied at the HTTP layer.

- cargo build --release    → silent (1 unused warning, harmless)
- cargo test --release      → 64/64 pass:
    37 unit + 20 smoke (including the 4 new intersect tests) + 7 HTTP

Closes the v0.6 sprint per the README "Sprint-sized" entry. Three

## v0.6a — mTLS auth mode

- New mtls.rs module:
  - MtlsConfig { server_cert_pem, server_key_pem, client_ca_pem }
  - build_acceptor() → tokio_rustls::TlsAcceptor with
    WebPkiClientVerifier (mandatory client cert, no allow_unauth)
  - cert_sha256_hex() — deterministic hex of DER cert bytes
  - principal_for_client_cert() → "mtls:<first-16-hex>"
- AuthMode::Mtls(Arc<MtlsConfig>) added.
- HTTP listener:
  - At startup, builds TlsAcceptor once (fail-fast on bad PEM).
  - Per-connection: TLS handshake → extract client cert chain →
    SHA-256 fingerprint. The fingerprint flows into the
    SessionBindings tuple as the third axis (with principal +
    client_id). Stolen JWT replayed from a different client cert →
    SessionDecision::Mismatch → 401.
- mTLS combines transparently with JWT: TLS at the edge gives
  transport-level identity, JWT carries app-level scopes; the JWT
  principal wins for capability mapping but the cert fingerprint
  still binds the session.
- 5 new unit tests (cert_sha256 deterministic + non-colliding;
  principal length; build_acceptor rejects empty PEM).

mcp-server v0.5 — RS256/ES256 + JWKS hot rotation + session binding

Closes the v0.5 sprint per the README "Sprint-sized" entry. Three
deliverables that complete the production-grade auth stack.

## v0.5a — RS256/ES256 JWT + JWKS hot rotation

- jsonwebtoken `use_pem` feature flipped on. JwtKey gains 3 new
  variants:
    JwtKey::RsaPem(Vec<u8>)   — RS256/384/512 via DecodingKey::from_rsa_pem
    JwtKey::EcPem(Vec<u8>)    — ES256/384 via DecodingKey::from_ec_pem
    JwtKey::Jwks(Arc<JwksKeys>) — kid-routed key rotation set
- New jwks.rs module:
  - JwksKeys: thread-safe HashMap<kid, DecodingKey> behind RwLock
  - spawn_refresh_task: initial sync fetch (fail-fast on bad URL),
    then background tokio task that GETs the JWKS URL on a
    configurable interval (default 5 min), parses RFC 7517 RSA
    keys via DecodingKey::from_rsa_components(n, e), hot-swaps
    into the rotation set
  - Verifier uses jsonwebtoken::decode_header to extract `kid`,
    looks up the DecodingKey for that kid, validates with it
- 6 unit tests cover empty/install/replace/parse/round-trip/spawn-task

## v0.5b — Session binding

- New sessions.rs module:
  - SessionBindings: 10k-LRU keyed on mcp-session-id header
  - SessionDecision::{Allowed{first_sighting}, Mismatch{...}}
  - First sighting records (session_id) → (principal, client_id,
    mtls_fingerprint); subsequent requests must match.
- HTTP middleware enforces session binding before reaching the rmcp
  service. A token presented under a different (principal, client_id)
  combo than the session was bound to returns 401.
  This is the SOTA reply to "stolen JWT replayed elsewhere".
- 7 unit tests cover empty session, first-sighting, second-sighting
  match, principal change, client_id change, mtls fingerprint change,
  post-eviction passes.

## CLI surface

main.rs gains 4 new flags + 4 new alg values:
  --jwt-rsa-pem-file PATH        (RS256/384/512)
  --jwt-ec-pem-file PATH         (ES256/384)
  --jwt-jwks-url URL             (production: hot rotation)

Implement ipfs-backend/ — bundle distribution over IPFS (ADR-005)

Seventh sibling crate. BackendAdapter that publishes ruLake bundles
(table.rulake.json) to IPFS and reads them back by CIDv1. Bundle-only
by design — vector bodies stay on the body-store backend (gcs/fs/local).

## Architecture

- Three operating modes (ADR-005 §2):
  - Mode::Kubo                       — kubo HTTP RPC; read+write+pin
  - Mode::Gateway                    — public gateway, read-only
  - Mode::KuboWithGatewayFallback    — kubo first, gateway on miss (audit-loud)
- Witness ↔ CID per ADR-005 §3 (two anchors, one cache key):
  - rvf_witness (SHAKE-256(32) hex) stays the cache anchor unchanged
  - data_ref becomes "ipfs://<cid>"
  - Generation::Opaque(cid) carries the CID
  - Existing variant-tag domain separation at src/bundle.rs:91-94 keeps
    GCS-Num and IPFS-Opaque generations from colliding by construction
- Cheap current_bundle() override per ADR-005 §4 + ADR-004 §Resources:
  - Fast path: operator pre-set CID + dim → no IPFS round trip
  - Otherwise: single-block ipfs cat (bundles ≤ 64 KiB by from_json's cap)
- pull_vectors() errors with ADR-005 §1 bundle-only message — operators
  wire IPFS for the witness, body backend for the data
- Bearer auth via kubo's native API.Authorizations HTTPAuthSecrets

## Library choice — pivoted from ADR-005's recommendation

ADR-005 §2 picked ipfs-api-backend-hyper. Real-world: it transitively
pulls multihash 0.17 → core2 0.4 (yanked). Pivoted to plain reqwest
against kubo's HTTP RPC (POST /api/v0/{add,cat,pin/add}). ~50 LOC,
~40 fewer transitive deps, no upstream maintenance dependency. The
SDK choice in ADR-005 §2 stays valid for v0.2 once the upstream
multihash chain unyanks; we noted the choice in ipfs-backend/README.md.

             current_bundle_fast_path_skips_network,

             gateway_only_mode_refuses_publish,
             list_collections_reflects_register)
    1 live test scaffolded behind RULAKE_IPFS_LIVE_TEST=1
- Mock kubo HTTP server in tests covers add/cat/pin paths end-to-end
- Bundle witness round-trip through publish→fetch is byte-exact

existing "First cloud backend" section, plus the ADR-005 reference
to the design-docs list.

Add ADR-005 — ipfs-backend + Compute Engine kubo deploy

Drafted by the goal-planner sub-agent in the background while the
v0.4 sprint closed in the foreground. 1306 lines matching ADR-004's
depth (the user's reference anchor).

Headline decisions:

- Crate: new `ipfs-backend/` sibling per ADR-001. Bundle-only —
  vector bodies stay on the original backend (Parquet on GCS, etc.).
  IPFS is the witness/bundle distribution layer, not the bulk-data
  layer.
- Library: `ipfs-api-backend-hyper` 0.6 talking kubo HTTP RPC.
  Rejects iroh (still pre-IPFS-compat per their April 2026 docs)
  and rust-ipfs (alpha). Three operating modes — `kubo` default,
  `gateway-only` (read-only/CI/no-network), `kubo + pinning-service`
  (Storacha/Pinata/Filebase) for redundancy.
- Witness ↔ CID: two anchors, one cache key. The SHAKE-256(32)
  rvf_witness stays the cache anchor unchanged. The IPFS CIDv1
  base32 lands in `Generation::Opaque(String)` and `data_ref`
  becomes `ipfs://<cid>`. Existing variant-tag domain separation
  at src/bundle.rs:91-94 keeps GCS-`Num` and IPFS-`Opaque`
  generations from colliding.
- Read path: single-block `ipfs cat` (no DAG walk; bundles are
  ≤ 64 KiB by from_json's cap). Honours ADR-004 §Resources cheap-
  current_bundle contract for free.
- Public-gateway fallback: opt-in, audit-loud. Excludes the
  decommissioned Cloudflare gateway. ipfs.io / dweb.link / w3s.link
  remain.
- Deployment annex: Compute Engine `e2-small`, pd-balanced 50 GB,
  no public IP, IAP-tunneled SSH, kubo bound to localhost +
  private-IP via firewall (`--source-tags rulake-serving`),
  bearer auth via kubo's native API.Authorizations HTTPAuthSecrets,
  daily PD snapshots. **~$20-25/mo at steady state.**
- Security: loud about public-by-default; AES-256-GCM envelope as
  the example for private bundles. The existing witness-fail-closed
  guard at src/bundle.rs:349 covers maliciously-pinned bundles for
  free (a poisoned bundle whose body doesn't hash to its claimed
  witness fails verify_witness — IPFS doesn't bypass the existing
  fail-closed posture).

mcp-server v0.4 complete — HTTP-side wiring + tools/list filter + README

Closes the v0.4 sprint. Wires the modules from ce3975d into the
HTTP middleware + adds the visibility filter the agent surface needs.

## HTTP middleware: full layered defense

http::handle now runs every request through four checks in order:
1. Auth — `none` / `bearer` / `jwt`. JWT verifies signature, iss,
   aud (RFC 8707), exp, and surfaces the request's scope→capability
   set as a debug log line (per-request CapabilitySet wiring lands
   in v0.5 alongside JWKS).
2. Replay protection — MCP-Request-Id LRU dedup over 10k window.
   409 Conflict on second sighting.
3. Layered rate limit — process bucket + (transport, principal)
   bucket here; per-(principal, backend, collection) fires inside
   the planner where routes are known. 429 on deny, with the
   denying layer in the audit log.
4. Hand off to rmcp's StreamableHttpService.

`serve()` keeps the v0.2 signature for compat; `serve_with_guards()`
exposes the replay + rate-limit Arcs for tests.

main.rs gains:

RS256/ES256 + JWKS fetch flagged for v0.5.

ServerHandler now overrides list_tools to filter by self.capabilities.
Single source of truth: required_cap_for_tool() in server.rs maps each
tool name to its required Capability. Used by both the visibility
filter AND the per-call require_cap gate (defense in depth).

  --capabilities read,publish      → 4 tools  (+ publish_bundle, refresh_from_bundle_dir)

mcp-server v0.4 SOTA security stack: RBAC + JWT + rate limit + replay

Lays the security foundation requested ("fully implement all
capabilities including RBAC and auth, SOTA"). Four new modules
plus enforcement wiring through the planner.

## v0.4a — per-collection RBAC (the headline ask)

- New `[[allow]]` blocks in mcp.toml: backend (exact), collection
  (anchored regex), caps. Empty list = unrestricted (v0.3 compat).
- `allow.rs` — compile-and-check engine. Read is implicitly granted
  by any matching block; Publish/Admin require explicit caps grant.
- Anchored regex: `docs.*` matches `docs.public` but NOT
  `secret-docs.public` (hardens against prefix-match exploits).
- Planner threads `Capability` through `resolve_routes_for_cap`;
  refresh intent uses Publish, search uses Read.
- Refusals carry typed `code: "ALLOWLIST_DENIED_*"` per route in
  the decision trace — audit can tell "no rule" vs "rule but no cap".
- 4 unit tests + 1 e2e (allowed route works, denied route refuses
  with PolicyRefusedAllowlist + per-route refusal code).

## v0.4b — JWT bearer auth (OAuth-style scope→capability)

- New `JwtAuth` validator: jsonwebtoken signature + iss + aud + exp.
  RFC 8707 Resource Indicators enforced via aud claim.
- v0.4 ships HMAC (HS256/384/512); RSA/EC public-key keys + JWKS
  fetch loop land in v0.5 with the `use_pem` feature flag flip.
- Scope/scp claim → CapabilitySet via documented map:
    mcp:rulake:read     → Read
    mcp:rulake:publish  → Read + Publish

Iteration 2 of the /loop. Two deliverables.

## mcp-server v0.2 (the deployability gate)

- New transport: Streamable HTTP via rmcp's StreamableHttpService +
  hyper 1.x + tower. Default bind 127.0.0.1:7440. DNS-rebinding guard
  via rmcp's allowed_hosts (loopback by default).
- Three auth modes wired (`--auth none|bearer`; oauth/mtls land in v0.3):
  - `none` — accepted on loopback. Public bind requires
    `--insecure-allow-no-auth` (intentionally embarrassing flag).
  - `bearer` — file-loaded token, constant-time compare via
    `subtle::ConstantTimeEq`. Audit principal is the token's short
    fingerprint. **Marked dev-only** per ADR-004 §5: public bind
    requires `--allow-bearer-on-public` AND emits a WARN every 60s
    while running.
- Subcommand split: `rulake-mcp stdio` (carried forward from v0.1) /
  `rulake-mcp http`.

## RVF-as-backend investigation (no code, doc only)

User asked "for Parquet could we use rvf?" — explored. Result:
strong on the merits (native RaBitQ codes, native witness chain,
append-only segment model), but rvf-runtime today has no public
iter_vectors() / read_vector(id) — only query() (returns
ID+distance, no vector content). The lower-level
read_path::read_vec_seg_payload is the right primitive but is
pub(crate). Per ADR-001 we don't fork the vendored upstream.

Documented in docs/research/rvf-backend-blocker.md with three real
paths (upstream PR, our own parser via rvf-types, or extend
ruLake's BackendAdapter trait with pull_prebuilt_index). The
gcs-backend (commit c706dc6) covers the cross-ecosystem case until
the upstream lands.

Add gcs-backend/ — Parquet-on-GCS BackendAdapter (ADR-155 §M2)

The first cloud backend. Reads vector columns from Parquet files on
Google Cloud Storage; cache coherence rides the existing GCS
per-object generation token (every reupload bumps the gen → the
witness changes → ruLake invalidates the cache, all via the
existing Generation::Num variant in src/bundle.rs).

Auth is Application Default Credentials — no bespoke auth code; an
operator runs `gcloud auth application-default login` or sets
GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS and the object_store crate handles
the rest.

Parquet schema contract (v0.1):
- id     INT64           non-null  (cast to u64 by bit pattern)
- vector LIST<FLOAT32>   non-null  (or FixedSizeList<FLOAT32, N>)

Cheap current_bundle() override — required by ADR-004 §Resources for
the rulake://bundle/{backend}/{collection} resource. Does a HEAD on
the object (~1 RTT) + a footer-only schema read (a few KiB) instead
of the default impl's full pull_vectors. The default impl would melt
a remote backend at resource-read rates.

Sibling crate per ADR-001 (no root workspace; depends on
ruvector-rulake via path = "..").

Architecture (ADR-004):

- Sibling crate at mcp-server/ per ADR-001 (no root workspace; the
  parent ruvector-rulake is reached via path = "..").
