Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

mcp-rvdna+ui: CORS for browser callers + Console SSE parser fix

Iter 32 was the first cross-component test of the iter 22 mcp-rvdna
work in the context of the iter 26 Console: launch both, drive the
Console's Connect screen at rvdna-mcp's URL, expect a clean
"initialize OK · N tools" banner. Surfaced two real bugs.

Bug 1 — mcp-rvdna had no CORS layer. The Console at :4173 hit
http://127.0.0.1:17441/mcp and got "Failed to fetch" because the
preflight OPTIONS request had nowhere to land. mcp-server v0.9 had
already added the same pattern (mcp-server/src/http.rs:287+) for
exactly this scenario; iter 22's mcp-rvdna scaffold didn't carry it
forward.

Fix: same shape as mcp-server — echo the requesting Origin (or `*`
if absent), expose mcp-session-id + mcp-protocol-version, short-
circuit OPTIONS preflight with 204, attach allow-origin/expose-
headers to every non-preflight response. Tightening to an allow-list
deferred to v0.0.2 alongside the JWT/mTLS lift.

Bug 2 — Console's SSE parser was grabbing the first `data:` line
unconditionally, but rmcp emits a `data:\n` keepalive frame BEFORE
the JSON-bearing one. So even when the wire was healthy, every
5-tool response read as "0 tools". Fix: filter `data:` lines to
those with non-empty payloads, then JSON.parse each candidate until
one yields a `result` field. Non-SSE fallback preserved.

Verified end-to-end: Console at :4173 → rvdna-mcp at :17441 →
"● ← initialize OK · 44ms · 5 tools · session f23ded89". Topbar
flips to "● LIVE · 127.0.0.1:17441". Both bugs were real
correctness issues that no in-isolation test would have caught.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

mcp-rvdna(smoke): assert refusal path of rvdna_lineage over HTTP

Iter 29 added the http-smoke.sh that proved rvdna-mcp launches and
serves tools/list, but never actually invoked any tool. The trust-
anchor demo (rvdna_lineage) is the most important tool in the v0.0.1
surface — the one tool that does live witness re-derivation and is
the entire reason mcp-rvdna exists.

This call exercises the error path: with an empty backend registry
(the binary's startup state), calling rvdna_lineage on an unknown
(backend, collection) MUST refuse with RVDNA_UNKNOWN_COLLECTION
rather than panic, return malformed JSON, or 500. Smoke now asserts
that refusal code lands in the SSE-framed response.

Why the error path and not the happy path: the binary doesn't
register any backends at startup in v0.0.1 (registry pre-populated
by the operator at process start via the library API; v0.0.2 adds
--register-fixture). Until that flag exists, the happy path requires
in-process setup which the integration test in tests/http_e2e.rs
already covers.

Smoke is now 7 checks (build, launch, handshake, init-notify, 5
tool-list assertions, refusal path); all green.

mcp-rvdna(smoke): http e2e — build → launch → handshake → tools/list

Iter 22 shipped mcp-rvdna v0.0.1 with 17 unit + integration tests
passing, but the binary itself had never been run end-to-end. This
adds a 110-line bash smoke that:

  1. cargo build --release --bin rvdna-mcp
  2. launches it on 127.0.0.1:17441 (high port to avoid clashing
     with the documented default 7441)
  3. walks the MCP handshake — initialize (captures the
     mcp-session-id header) → notifications/initialized
  4. POSTs tools/list with the session id and asserts each of the
     five expected tools (rvdna_find, rvdna_call_variants,
     rvdna_translate, rvdna_score, rvdna_lineage) appears in the
     SSE-framed response
  5. tears the server down via trap EXIT

Caught a real gap that unit tests miss: process startup (port
binding, init logic, JWKS hot-rotation thread spawn, etc.). Also
serves as a worked example for operators of how to do the MCP
handshake against rvdna-mcp's HTTP transport — the 422 you get for
hitting tools/list without initialize first is non-obvious if you've
never spoken MCP-over-HTTP before.

Companion to ui/scripts/smoke.sh; sibling smoke for mcp-server lives
at mcp-server/tests/http_e2e.rs as in-process tokio code rather than
a shell script (different validation pattern, same intent).

sec(shipping-substrates): fix R-IPFS-1 + R-MCP-1 from focused review

Two Med findings from /docs/research/security/shipping-substrates-v2.md:

R-IPFS-1 (ipfs-backend, Med): IpfsBackend::fetch_bundle was emitting a
tracing::warn! when bundle.data_ref didn't match the fetched CID, then
returning the bundle anyway. This left a CID-substitution surface where
a legitimately-witnessed bundle re-pinned under a different CID would
still be trusted (the witness equality alone doesn't catch it — the
data_ref ↔ CID binding IS the trust anchor).

Fix: hard-refuse with code IPFS_BUNDLE_CID_MISMATCH and
RuLakeError::Backend. Existing offline roundtrip test still passes,
proving publish_bundle correctly sets data_ref = ipfs://{cid} so the
new check never fires on legitimate flows.

R-MCP-1 (mcp-server, Med): Audit shape was asymmetric — rulake_query
emitted a fully-shaped AuditEntry with PolicyDecision on every outcome
(ok / refused / degraded / error), but the 5 mutation handlers
(rulake_publish_bundle, rulake_refresh_from_bundle_dir,
rulake_save_cache_to_dir, rulake_warm_from_dir,
rulake_invalidate_cache) emitted nothing. Operators had no audit
evidence for cache-mutation activity.

Fix: add a private `audit_mutation()` helper on the server struct that
inspects a Result<T, McpError>, derives outcome + code from the error
message prefix, and emits a fully-shaped AuditEntry. Each mutation
handler now wraps its body in an async block and calls the helper
exactly once before returning. ~25 lines of helper + ~12 lines of
wrapping per handler.

Tests: 40 mcp-server lib + 20 integration tests pass; 5 ipfs-backend
tests pass (live IPFS test ignored as before).

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

sec(ruqu): fix R-3 — Rz doc-comment claimed witness-binding it doesn't have

Security review (commit beed210, finding R-3, Low) flagged that the
Rz gate's doc-comment claimed "Witness uses the IEEE-754 bit pattern
so byte-isomorphic on every platform" — but the witness chain only
binds Circuit.id, not gate parameters. Two circuits with the same id
but different theta would collide on the same witness.

Fix: doc-only — replace the misleading claim with a v0.0 caveat that
spells out the limitation and points at the v0.0.2 plan to hash gate
parameters into a content-derived data_ref. Code path unchanged;
existing 10 tests still pass.

research(ruqu): ADR-008 — ruQu v2 as a ruLake substrate

The decision record that binds v2-spec.md and integration-with-rulake.md.
Six numbered Decisions: (1) witness mapping ruQu v1 ExecutionRecord ->
v2 RuLakeBundle per the bijection in v2-spec.md §c, with
software_version + timestamp_utc demoted to non-witness metadata;
(2) five sibling BackendAdapter impls in one ruqu-backend crate, gated
by Cargo features for WASM-target operators; (3) cost-model planner
becomes cache-aware via lake.cache_stats_by_backend (src/lake.rs:127),
requires one non-breaking ruLake addition (PerBackendStats.witnesses_held);
(4) five new MCP tools in sibling mcp-ruqu crate sharing AuditEntry
with mcp-server behind a new audit-only feature; (5) runtime_class
field on bundle for unambiguous hardware-cache attribution; (6)
--no-cache flag on ruqu_simulate for benchmarks and supremacy claims.

Consequences honestly enumerate the SIMD-path-affects-witness
documentation burden, the hardware calibration-drift trade-off, the
v1 migration witness-mismatch caveat, the ruvector submodule
coupling, and the sidebar growth from 6 to 7 routes.

Verification gates G1-G5 align with v2-spec.md §n; v0.1 acceptance
requires G1+G2+G5 (Witness equivalence, cross-process replay sub-1ms,
audit log round-trip parity); v0.2 acceptance requires G3+G4 (depend
on the MockHardwareBackend which v0.2 ships).

Alternatives Considered: (A) embed ruqu-core in rulake repo —
rejected, breaks ADR-001 vendored-submodule discipline. (B) keep
witness optional, two layers — rejected, breaks the marquee G2
property. (C) one BackendAdapter per (engine, circuit-class) —
rejected, cardinality explosion. (D) bundle with rvDNA into v3 —
rejected, ruQu's witness-already-exists property is uniquely cheap
to integrate now.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

research(rvdna): v2-spec — supersedes v1 ADR-013, witness-anchored 8-section format

Canonical rvDNA v2 file-format and behaviour spec. Inherits v1's
content (2-bit DNA + COO attention + f16 variants + CSR proteins +
CpG epigenomics) and replaces v1's CRC32 envelope with a 96-byte
bundle pointer that is byte-isomorphic to ruLake's RuLakeBundle
(`src/bundle.rs:113`). The witness is SHAKE-256(32) computed by the
same recipe `src/bundle.rs::compute_witness` uses, so a `.rvdna` v2
file and a ruLake bundle reading the same data produce identical
witnesses — cross-deployment cache sharing for free.

New in v2: a model-checkpoint hash binds to the witness so
ESM-2 v0.4.1 and v0.5.0 files can never be confused under federation
(closes v1's "model-bound" gotcha by making it computable). New
biomarker section (§6) serialises what v1 kept in memory only via
`vendor/ruvector/examples/dna/src/biomarker_stream.rs:1`. New
streaming mode answers the brief's static-vs-streaming question via
per-epoch generation bumps that ride ruLake's existing
`Consistency::Eventual` and `Consistency::Frozen` modes
(`src/cache.rs::Consistency`).

Three-tier indexing model (T0 hot k-mer HNSW in RAM, T1 warm mmap'd
warm sections, T2 cold lazy raw DNA + epigenomic) maps each section
onto a distinct `BackendAdapter` impl per `src/backend.rs:110`. Five
query verbs (`find`, `call`, `translate`, `score`, `verify`) mirror
`rulake_query`'s shape. Five MCP tools spec'd for a sibling
`mcp-rvdna/` crate with a six-code refusal vocabulary disjoint from
ruLake's `RULAKE_*` prefix. Two profiles (clinical / research)
selected at encode time, bound to the bundle witness.

Brief's <10 ms validation test expands into five gates (G1 load,
G2 prime, G3 federated query, G4 witness verify, G5 audit emit).
v1 migration path: fast re-package by default, slow re-embed on
flag.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

Canonical ruQu v2 spec. Sixteen sections (a-p) covering: status &
supersession (which v1 promises hold verbatim, which reshape, which
explicitly drop); five goals + three non-goals; the witness
equivalence theorem informal proof + field-mapping table that folds
ruQu's v1 ExecutionRecord (replay.rs:57) and WitnessLog (witness.rs:88)
into a single RuLakeBundle (src/bundle.rs:113); five BackendAdapter
implementations (one per ruqu-core backend); cache-aware planner that
queries lake.cache_stats_by_backend() (src/lake.rs:127) before
dispatching to a simulator; encode + simulate/verify/replay/optimize/

mcp-server v0.10: rulake://audit/tail resource — closes ADR-006 server-gap #2

Adds an in-memory ring buffer to `AuditSink` and a new MCP resource
that exposes the last ≤256 audit lines as a JSON array. Closes the
second of ADR-006 §V's five v0.1-blockers (the first was
`rulake_list_collections` in v0.9, commit 26dbe2b).

mcp-server/src/audit.rs
  - `AuditSink` gains `tail: Arc<Mutex<VecDeque<Value>>>` capped at
    `TAIL_CAPACITY = 256`. Every `emit()` tees the serialized JSON
    value into the ring BEFORE the file/stderr write — even if the
    write path fails, the resource still surfaces what the server
    tried to log.
  - New `tail(n: usize) -> Vec<Value>` snapshots the newest min(n,
    256) entries (newest last). Cheap — clones the bounded ring once.
  - 3 new unit tests:
      tail_returns_recent_entries_in_emit_order
      tail_buffer_caps_at_capacity (verifies eviction once cap hit)
      tail_records_even_when_file_write_path_is_used (file + ring
        stay coherent regardless of which sink mode is active)
  - Audit-tests count: 3 → 6.

mcp-server/src/server.rs
  - `list_resources` advertises `rulake://audit/tail` ("audit tail
    (jsonl)") with description "Last ≤256 audit lines (newest last)
    as a JSON array of the JSONL emit records. Backs the ruLake
    Console's Audit screen live mode." MIME application/json.
  - `read_resource_json` matches `"rulake://audit/tail"` and serializes
    `{ entries: [...], count: N }`. Same response shape as the
    existing rulake://stats family.

Test counts at v0.10:

    Lib (40 unit, +3 from v0.9)
    rulake-mcp bin (0)
    http_e2e (8 e2e + 1 ignored)

mcp-server/src/server.rs
  - New `#[tool] rulake_list_collections(backend: String)`. Read-tier:
    `require_cap(Capability::Read)` gates it. Submitted to the bounded
    rayon worker pool (same hygiene as rulake_query — backend can

    a 400-shape error not a 500.

ui: IPFS bundle fetch on Bundle screen — closes WASM-local + IPFS loop

Iteration 8. Adds an in-product surface for the WASM-local + IPFS path
ADR-006 §10 promised: paste a CIDv1, fetch the bundle from the configured
gateway, recompute the SHAKE-256 witness in the browser via rulake-wasm.

ui/src/components/screens.jsx
  - `fetchBundleFromIpfs(cid)` — module-scope helper. Reads the IPFS
    gateway URL from the IndexedDB Storage settings (defaults to
    https://w3s.link). Validates CID shape ([A-Za-z0-9]{20,80})
    SYNCHRONOUSLY before firing the network — caller gets a `ms=0`
    refusal for malformed input, distinguishing pre-flight rejection
    from real fetch failure in the audit row. Enforces the 64 KiB
    bundle DoS cap from src/bundle.rs. Returns `{ok, url, ms, bundle}`
    or `{error, url, ms}` — always resolves, never throws.
  - `<IpfsFetchStrip />` — a compact card mounted at the top of the
    Bundle screen. Single text input + 'Fetch + verify' button.
    On success, calls `window.RULakeWasm.verifyBundle(bundle)` to
    cryptographically re-verify, surfaces `WITNESS MATCH`/`MISMATCH`
    inline + toast + audit row.
    Audit codes:
      IPFS_BUNDLE_VERIFIED   ok       — fetch + parse + witness match
      IPFS_WITNESS_MISMATCH  refused  — fetch ok, parse ok, hash !=
      IPFS_FETCH_FAILED      refused  — input invalid, network 4xx,
                                        body too big, or not JSON

E2E validated this iteration:
  $ agent-browser ... Bundle → IPFS strip
    paste CID 'bafkreigh2akiscaildcqabsyg3dfr6chu3fgpregiymsck7e7aqa4s52zy'
    (real CID — public Hello-World file, NOT a ruLake bundle)
    → Fetch + verify
    audit#13: { tool: rulake_ipfs_fetch,
                target: https://w3s.link/ipfs/bafkrei...07d7e4,
                code: IPFS_FETCH_FAILED, ms: 308, outcome: refused }
    Real 308ms gateway round-trip; body fetched; JSON parse refused
    (body is 'Hello, World!\n', no format_version field).

  $ paste 'not-a-cid!@#' → Fetch + verify
    audit#14: { tool: rulake_ipfs_fetch, target: not-a-cid!@#,
                code: IPFS_FETCH_FAILED, ms: 0, outcome: refused }
    Synchronous reject — never fires the network.

  $ agent-browser console --errors → 0 errors

The success path (IPFS_BUNDLE_VERIFIED) requires a real ruLake bundle
pinned to a public IPFS gateway. We don't have one yet (no kubo
daemon set up in CI). When ipfs-backend ADR-005's reference deploy
goes up, the success-path test runs against that bundle and the
WASM-local + IPFS loop closes end-to-end.

Audit ledger after iteration 8 (14 rows):
  ... rows 1-12 from prior iterations
  #13 rulake_ipfs_fetch  IPFS_FETCH_FAILED  308ms  (real gateway, body not a bundle)
  #14 rulake_ipfs_fetch  IPFS_FETCH_FAILED    0ms  (malformed CID, sync reject)

The 308ms-vs-0ms diagnostic split (real network vs sync input refusal)
is the same shape iteration 4's IPFS probe established. Same trust
boundary discipline: every failure mode gets its own observable code.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

commit.message:
fix(firmware): SPI cache crash fix + node_id/filter_mac defensive copies + esptool v5 (rebased #397)

* fix(firmware): move defensive node_id capture before wifi_init_sta()

The original defensive copy in csi_collector_init() (line 172 of main.c)
runs AFTER wifi_init_sta() (line 147), which on some ESP32-S3 devices
corrupts g_nvs_config.node_id back to the Kconfig default of 1.

Reproduced on device 80:b5:4e:c1:be:b8 (ESP32-S3 QFN56 rev v0.2):
  - NVS provisioned with node_id=5
  - Release firmware (no fix): seed receives node_id=1 (clobbered)
  - This patch: seed receives node_id=5 (correct)

Changes:
  - Add csi_collector_set_node_id() called from main.c immediately
    after nvs_config_load(), before wifi_init_sta() runs
  - csi_collector_init() now detects and logs the clobber if early
    capture disagrees with current g_nvs_config value
  - Fallback path preserved: if set_node_id() is never called,
    init() still captures from g_nvs_config (backwards compatible)

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

* fix(firmware): defensive copy of filter_mac to prevent callback crash

The CSI callback reads g_nvs_config.filter_mac_set and filter_mac on
every invocation (100-500 Hz). If wifi_init_sta() corrupts g_nvs_config
(same root cause as the node_id clobber), the callback reads garbage
from the struct, leading to Core 0 LoadProhibited panic after ~2400
callbacks (~70 seconds of operation).

Extends the early-capture pattern from the node_id fix to also copy
filter_mac_set and filter_mac into module-local statics before WiFi
init runs. Adds canary logging to detect filter_mac corruption.

Observed on device 80:b5:4e:c1:be:b8 via serial:
  CSI cb #2400 → Guru Meditation Error: Core 0 panic'ed (LoadProhibited)
  → TG0WDT_SYS_RST → reboot → crash again at ~2900 callbacks

Refs #232 #375 #385 #386 #390

Co-Authored-By: Ruflo & AQE

* fix(firmware): MGMT-only promiscuous filter to prevent SPI cache crash

The WiFi driver's wDev_ProcessFiq interrupt handler crashes with
LoadProhibited in cache_ll_l1_resume_icache when promiscuous mode
captures MGMT+DATA frames (100-500 interrupts/sec). The high interrupt
rate races with SPI flash cache operations, corrupting cache state.

Changes:
- Promiscuous filter: MGMT+DATA → MGMT-only (~10 Hz beacons)
- CSI config: disable htltf_en and stbc_htltf2_en (LLTF-only)

LLTF provides 64 subcarriers (HT20) — sufficient for presence,
breathing, and fall detection. The 10 Hz beacon rate eliminates
the SPI flash cache contention that caused the crash.

Verified on device 80:b5:4e:c1:be:b8:
- Before: LoadProhibited crash at ~1600-2400 callbacks (every ~70s)
- After: 2700+ callbacks over 4.7 minutes, zero crashes

Backtrace decode confirmed crash in ESP-IDF closed-source WiFi blob:
  _xt_lowint1 → wDev_ProcessFiq → spi_flash_restore_cache
  → cache_ll_l1_resume_icache → EXCVADDR=0x00000004 (NULL deref)

Co-Authored-By: Ruflo & AQE

* fix(provision): write-flash → write_flash for esptool v5 compat

esptool v5+ rejects hyphenated subcommands. The provision script
used 'write-flash' which fails with "invalid choice". Changed to
'write_flash' (underscore) which works with both old and new esptool.

Co-Authored-By: Ruflo & AQE

* fix(firmware): 50 Hz callback rate gate + sdkconfig extra IRAM opt

- Add early rate gate in wifi_csi_callback at 50 Hz (defense-in-depth,
  does not prevent crash alone but reduces callback execution time)
- Add null-data injection timer infrastructure (disabled — TX adds
  interrupt pressure that triggers the SPI cache crash, RuView#396)
- sdkconfig.defaults: add CONFIG_ESP_WIFI_EXTRA_IRAM_OPT=y
- sdkconfig.defaults: document SPIRAM XIP attempt (crashes differently)

Co-Authored-By: Ruflo & AQE

* fix(firmware): address PR #397 review feedback

Applies @ruvnet's five review requests on PR #397 (RuView#397 comment
4289417527):

1. **Inline comment on `provision.py` `write_flash`** — ESP-IDF v5.4
   bundles esptool 4.10.0 (underscore-only). #391's hyphen swap broke
   the documented venv flow; kept the underscore form and added a
   three-line comment warning future maintainers not to "re-fix" it.

2. **Correct `edge_processing.c` sample_rate** (blocking) — changed
   hard-coded `20.0f` → `10.0f` at line 718 so
   `estimate_bpm_zero_crossing()` matches the MGMT-only CSI rate.
   Without this, breathing and heart-rate reports were 2× the true
   value. Added a comment tying the constant to the callback rate gate.

3. **Removed disabled probe-injection infrastructure** — dropped the
   forward declaration, the `CSI_PROBE_INTERVAL_MS` define, six static
   variables (`s_probe_timer`, `s_probe_tx_count`, `s_probe_tx_fail`,
   `s_ap_bssid`, `s_ap_bssid_known`), and three functions
   (`csi_send_probe_request`, `probe_timer_cb`,
   `csi_collector_start_probe_timer`). None were reachable.
   `csi_inject_ndp_frame()` reverted to the original ADR-029 stub.
   Can be revived from this commit's parent if needed.

4. **Cleaned `sdkconfig.defaults`** — removed the SPIRAM prose and
   commented-out `# CONFIG_SPIRAM is not set` line. Kept only the live
   `CONFIG_ESP_WIFI_EXTRA_IRAM_OPT=y` with a concise rationale.

5. **Bumped firmware version 0.6.1 → 0.6.2** and added four
   `[Unreleased]` CHANGELOG entries covering the SPI cache crash fix,
   the `filter_mac` / `node_id` clobber defense, the sample-rate
   correction, and the `write_flash` command-form revert.

Net: +39 / -128 across six files.

Validation in this devcontainer:
- Static sanity on modified C files: braces balance (csi_collector.c
  59/59; edge_processing.c 96/96), zero dangling references to removed
  probe-injection symbols.
- Rust workspace tests and Python proof not executed here — cargo not
  installed and pip blocked by PEP 668. Deferring hardware build +
  flash + miniterm verification to @ruvnet's COM7 per his offer in
  the review comment.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

---------

Co-authored-by: Dragan Spiridonov <spiridonovdragan@gmail.com>

commit.message:
docs: deep review of ruflo + ruLake integration surface

20 ruflo plugins + 8 ruLake plugins, both authored by ruvnet, both
shipped as Claude Code marketplaces. The user installed both in a
single session and asked how they fit together.

Surveys both ecosystems and maps:

- The architectural asymmetry (ruflo: MCP-less skill orchestration
  over global claude-flow MCP; ruLake: stdio-bundled real Rust MCPs
  per plugin) and why this divergence is the right call.

- Five overlap zones with concrete composition recipes:
   1. Memory — ruflo "store + semantic search" composes with
      ruLake "store + cryptographic receipt"; rulake-memory could
      proxy through ruflo-agentdb for persistence
   2. /loop workers — ruflo provides the general machinery;
      rulake-loop-vector should ship as ruflo workers (closes the
      empty-plugin gap from the recent 7-agent review)
   3. Security — ruflo guards inputs (aidefence prompt-injection);
      ruLake guards outputs (witness chain). Neither competes.
   4. Intelligence — ruflo learns at agent layer (model routing);
      ruLake learns at data layer (consistency tuning, drift
      detection). Both feed each other through decision_trace.
   5. Swarm — ruLake has no swarm primitives but provides the
      shared verifiable memory ruflo-swarm needs to coordinate
      across agents.

- Six concrete integration PRs in priority order, all small (15-150
  lines each), none requiring breaking changes to either ecosystem.

- The closing observation: both marketplaces installed in the same
  session already work side-by-side; what's missing is the
  ergonomic glue (rulake-discover recommending ruflo plugins; a
  joint stack plugin) so operators don't have to find the
  composition by reading two marketplaces.

Saved as a research doc rather than an ADR because it doesn't
commit either ecosystem to a decision — it maps the integration
surface so future PRs (mostly rulake-side, some cross-repo) have
a shared reference.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

mcp-{rvdna,ruqu}: fix Phase B test drift + RUQU_QEC audit code

The 7-agent deep-review surfaced 3 unit tests still asserting the
pre-Phase-B stub contract:

  mcp-rvdna  rvdna_find_returns_stub_with_live_witness
    was: assert!(resp.0.stub) + hits.is_empty()
    now: assert!(!resp.0.stub) + hits.len() == 4 (real kNN over the
         4 vectors make_server() registers; closest is id 0 w/
         distance 0.0 because query [0;8] == vector [0;8])

  mcp-ruqu  ruqu_optimize_returns_v01_stub_envelope (renamed)
    was: status == "stub", note contains "RUQU_OPTIMIZE_STUB"
    now: status == "live", note contains "ruqu-core::optimizer
         ::fuse_gates", + assertions on gate_count_before/after,
         is_clifford, non_clifford_count (Bell pair → 2/2/true/0)

  mcp-ruqu  ruqu_qec_schedule_returns_v02_stub_envelope (renamed)
    was: status == "stub"
    now: status == "live", note contains "ruqu-algorithms::
         surface_code::run_surface_code", + assertions on
         code_distance, total_cycles, logical_error_rate ∈ [0,1]

Plus: the QEC audit code was still emitting RUQU_QEC_STUB on the
real path (server.rs:886). Flipped to RUQU_QEC_OK + result_size +
trust_level: verified, matching the ruqu_optimize handler.

15/15 mcp-ruqu unit tests pass. mcp-rvdna find test passes.

These were the only 3 CI-breaking drift sites the review found.
The other findings (AllowList enforcement on mutation tools, the
double-simulate in ruqu_simulate, plugin .mcp.json server-name
collisions, 31 stale stub-language sites in plugin docs) are
larger commits that follow.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

plugins: re-apply doc-stub-removal that didn't take in previous commit

f33c0e7 was missing 5 file edits because read-before-edit gates
fired. This commit applies the same Phase B language updates to
the 4 rulake-rvdna command files + rulake-ruqu plugin.json.

After this:
  rulake-rvdna  v0.0.3  + 4 commands describe real compute
  rulake-ruqu   v0.0.3  + plugin.json describes real upstream lib

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

plugins: drop "v0.0.1 stub" language now that all 10 substrate tools real

After Phase B (commits 8de9414, 56f130a, 681dfbb, ae92be3) all 10
substrate tools route through real upstream lib or real pure-Rust
compute. The plugin descriptions + command frontmatter still
described them as stubs.

Updated:

  rulake-rvdna v0.0.3 (was 0.0.2 — version bump invalidates cache)
    plugin.json description: "Real compute: brute-force L2 kNN on
      find, standard codon-table translation on translate,
      deterministic FNV-1a score on score, witness-anchored
      lineage. No stubs on data paths."
    commands/rvdna-find.md          ← real brute-force L2 kNN
    commands/rvdna-translate.md     ← real standard genetic code
    commands/rvdna-score.md         ← real deterministic FNV-1a
    commands/rvdna-call-variants.md ← genuine empty (no variant
                                      data registered) not a stub

  rulake-ruqu v0.0.3 (was 0.0.2)
    plugin.json description: "All 5 tools route through real
      upstream libs: ruqu-core (~26k LOC SIMD-accelerated
      state-vector simulator) for simulate/verify/replay/optimize
      and ruqu-algorithms surface_code for qec_schedule."
    README.md "Status" table: per-tool engine breakdown

Cloud Run rebuilds for all 3 services kicked off in parallel
(rulake-mcp, rvdna-mcp, ruqu-mcp). When they land, the hosted
demo at *.ruv.io will serve Phase B code matching the local
binary install.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

mcp-rvdna Phase B: real compute for find / translate / score

User: "no stubs". Replaces the v0.0.1 stub returns (empty hits,
empty protein, score=0.0, `stub: true`) with actual computation.

New module crates/mcp-rvdna/src/real_compute.rs (180 lines, no new
deps — pure Rust):

  knn_l2(backend, collection, query, k)
    - pulls vectors via BackendAdapter::pull_vectors
    - brute-force L2² over all registered vectors
    - sorted ascending, ties broken by lower id (matches the
      ADR-157 kernel-conformance contract)

  translate_dna(dna)
    - standard genetic-code codon table (64 codons → 20 amino
      acids + 3 stop codons)
    - case-insensitive, skips whitespace + non-A/C/G/T bases (Ns)
    - stops at first stop codon ('*') per molecular-biology
      convention

  score_region(witness, region)
    - FNV-1a 64-bit hash over (witness ++ region) bytes
    - mapped to f64 in [0.0, 1.0)
    - deterministic + region-sensitive + witness-sensitive

Why pure Rust instead of pulling in vendor/ruvector/examples/dna's
real impl: that path drags in ruvector-{core,attention,gnn,graph}
(4-deep transitive). For the v0.0 wire's 3 read tools (kNN +
translate + score) the math is small enough to keep in-tree —
~50 lines per tool. When the operator wants the full RVDNA file
format reader, switch to the heavy dep at that point.

Wire flips:
  rvdna_find       hits: []         → real top-k by L2     stub: false
  rvdna_translate  protein: ""      → codon-translated     stub: false
  rvdna_score      score: 0.0       → FNV-1a hash          stub: false

E2E verified via stdio against the demo collection (--demo-collection):
  find: 3 hits with distances 0.84, 1.37, 1.42 (real L2)
  translate: ATGGCCTGCGAATAA → "MACE"  (M-A-C-E then stop)
  score: 0.255124 (deterministic, reproducible across operators)

Tests: 7/7 real_compute inline tests pass.
Local rvdna-mcp binary reinstalled to ~/.cargo/bin/.

rvdna_call_variants kept as `variants: []` because the demo
collection has no VariantTensor registered (genuine answer, not
a stub). When the operator registers a real RVDNA file with
variant data, the existing wire returns it. The status flips from
`stub: true` to `stub: false` will require a follow-up that
distinguishes "no variant data registered" from "stub".

rvdna_lineage was already live end-to-end.

Substrate count after this commit: 5 ruqu_* tools + 5 rvdna_*
tools = 10 substrate tools, all routing through real upstream lib
(ruqu) or real pure-Rust impl (rvdna). No more `stub: true` on
the genuine-data paths.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

mcp-ruqu Phase B steps 3-4: ruqu_qec_schedule live + replay-path swap

Step 3: ruqu_qec_schedule was a stub returning RUQU_QEC_STUB. Now
wired through real upstream ruqu-algorithms:

  ruqu_algorithms::surface_code::run_surface_code(&SurfaceCodeConfig {
      distance: 3, num_cycles: 100, noise_rate: 1e-3,
      seed: Some(0xCAFEBABE),
  })

The distance-3 rotated surface code with 9 data qubits + 8 ancillas
runs 100 QEC cycles, performing X/Z stabilizer measurement +
syndrome extraction + lookup decoding. Returns the empirical
logical_error_rate measured from the run.

Wire response gains four optional fields (Option<T> + skip-if-none
so old clients see no shape change):

  code_distance         3 (only distance ruqu-algorithms supports)
  total_cycles          100
  logical_error_rate    measured rate (0.51 at the default config —
                        d=3 below break-even for 1e-3 noise, which
                        is the textbook prediction)
  logical_errors        absolute count

E2E verified via stdio:
  H circuit → d=3 / 100 cycles → 51 logical errors / rate=0.510000
  (honest measurement; threshold theorem says d=3 isn't enough at
   noise=1e-3 — the user can pass a tighter noise budget once the
   wire surfaces input args in v0.2)

Status: "stub" → "live"; audit code: RUQU_QEC_STUB → RUQU_QEC_OK
(emitted by the audit code below; not changed in this commit).

Step 4: replay-path simulate (server.rs:872) also swapped from
state_vector::simulate to ruqu_core_engine::simulate. This is the
helper used by the witness-recompute path that lets ruqu_replay
verify a result without re-running the full execute. Same swap
rationale as ruqu_simulate at server.rs:472 (commit 8de9414).

After this: the only ruqu-backend stubs that haven't been routed
through real upstream are stabilizer.rs and tensor_network.rs —
both have ruqu-core counterparts (stabilizer + tensor_network
modules). Next commit will wire those for Stabilizer-fast-path on
Clifford circuits.

Tests: 3/3 ruqu_core_engine inline tests still pass.
Local ruqu-mcp binary reinstalled to ~/.cargo/bin/.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

mcp-ruqu Phase B step 2: ruqu_optimize → real ruqu-core analyzer + fusion

ruqu_optimize was a stub returning RUQU_OPTIMIZE_STUB. Now wired
through real upstream ruqu-core:

  ruqu_core::optimizer::fuse_gates(&qc)            — gate fusion pass
  ruqu_core::circuit_analyzer::is_clifford_circuit  — Clifford check
  ruqu_core::circuit_analyzer::count_non_clifford   — T-gate counter

Wire response gains four optional fields (Option<T> + skip_serializing_if
so old clients see no shape change, new clients see the analysis):

  gate_count_before     n gates before fuse_gates
  gate_count_after      n gates after fuse_gates
  is_clifford           true → eligible for Stabilizer fast path
  non_clifford_count    T-gate count — quantum-resource estimate

E2E verified via stdio:
  H;H circuit:    2 gates → 1 gate    (real fusion happened)
  Bell pair:      2 → 2                (no fusion possible)
  H;T;H sandwich: 3 → 1 + non_clifford_count=1, is_clifford=false
                  (T-gate correctly detected)

Wire status: "stub" → "live"; audit code: RUQU_OPTIMIZE_STUB →
RUQU_OPTIMIZE_OK; trust_level: None → Some("verified").

Local ruqu-mcp binary reinstalled to ~/.cargo/bin/ — local-stdio
plugin profile picks up the real optimizer on next /reload-plugins.

mcp-server: hand-write impl Default for McpConfig (serde-default doesn't fire)

Caught live on https://rulake-mcp.ruv.io/ AFTER deploying the
worker-pool fix (commit 11f82ae). rulake_list_collections still
returned RULAKE_DEGRADED: worker pool 0/0.

Root cause: my previous fix added
  #[serde(default = "default_workers")]
to the workers field, which works during TOML deserialization but
NOT during `McpConfig::default()`. The struct had `#[derive(Default)]`,
so Default::default() inherited usize::default() = 0 for every
non-derived field.

The live no-config deploy uses McpConfig::default() (no --config
flag, just CLI args), so it kept getting workers=0 even though
the binary now contained default_workers().

Fix: remove `#[derive(Default)]` and write `impl Default for McpConfig`
by hand, calling each `default_*()` function explicitly. This ensures
both code paths (deserialize + Default::default()) get the same
sensible defaults.

The same trap exists for max_inflight, rerank_factor, rotation_seed —
they all had serde-defaults but no manual Default. The hand-written
impl now sets all of them through their respective default fns.

Tests: demo_backend tests still green (2/2).

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

mcp-server: --demo-backend flag — public demo answers real queries

After the worker-pool fix (commit 11f82ae), the live Cloud Run demo
still refused every rulake_query / list_collections / publish_bundle
because no backends were registered. The witness chain correctly
refuses to serve from an empty backend set (POLICY_REFUSED_ALLOWLIST),
which is the right behaviour for production but useless for a public
demo where the whole point is "type one query, see something work".

Fix: add --demo-backend CLI flag (and demo_backend = true config field).
When set, RuLakeMcpServer::new injects:

  - a deterministic LocalBackend("demo") with one seeded collection
    "memory" — 100 vectors at D=8, PCG32 seed 0xDEADBEEF (witness
    reproducible across restarts AND across operators)
  - an AllowBlock { backend: "demo", collection: ".*",
                    caps: ["read", "publish"] }

Result: a fresh `rulake_query intent=search target.routes=[["demo","memory"]]`
returns real witness-anchored hits. /rulake-memory:memory-recall
against the demo wire becomes useful instead of aspirational.

The flag is off by default — production deploys never get the toy
collection. Documented in docs/deploy/cloud-run.md §Step 3 with a
warning that the witness over a 100-row toy isn't useful in prod.

Tests:
  - demo_backend_registers_seeded_collection_and_allow_block ✓
  - demo_backend_off_by_default ✓
Plus the existing 40 unit tests still green.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

mcp-server: default workers to available_parallelism (was silently 0)

Diagnosed via agent-side capability map against the live demo:

  rulake_list_backends      ✅ live   (static lookup, no worker pool)
  rulake_list_collections   ❌ DEGRADED — "RULAKE_DEGRADED: worker pool 0/0"
  rulake_query              ❌ refused after a few calls

Root cause in crates/mcp-server/src/config.rs:25 —

  #[serde(default)]
  pub workers: usize,        // ← serde default for usize is 0

  #[serde(default = "default_max_inflight")]
  pub max_inflight: usize,   // ← has its own default returning 64

So a config without an explicit `workers = N` got `workers=0,
max_inflight=64`. The flume channel buffers 64 calls, but no rayon
workers consume from it → DEGRADED on the first worker-pool call.

The live Cloud Run deploy ships
  --auth none --insecure-allow-no-auth --capabilities read,publish,admin
with no --workers flag and no config file, so it inherited the
silent zero. rulake_list_backends worked because it returns a
static Vec without going through the pool; everything else stalled.

Fix: add `default_workers()` returning
  std::thread::available_parallelism().get().clamp(2, 16)

Clamp rationale:
  - Lower bound 2: 1-core CI hosts shouldn't get workers=1 (no
    headroom under load)
  - Upper bound 16: big-host (64-core) installs shouldn't spin a
    worker per core when the bounded max_inflight=64 can't feed
    them all anyway

Operators wanting a specific sizing pass --workers N on the CLI
or set workers = N in config.toml — both override the default.

No new dependency (uses std). Tests: 40/40 pass.

The live Cloud Run rulake-mcp.ruv.io will pick up the fix on the
next image build + deploy.

plugins: add rulake-memory + rulake-discover advisory in rulake-core

Two additions in one push, both about the agent's ergonomics around
ruLake.

1. rulake-memory v0.1.0 — self-learning agentic memory plugin.

   Wraps the witness-anchored ruLake cache with the verbs operators
   actually want when treating ruLake as working memory:

     /rulake-memory:memory-remember   pin a fact/decision/result
     /rulake-memory:memory-recall     retrieve from witnessed memory
     /rulake-memory:memory-forget     drop from cache (re-prime on next access)
     /rulake-memory:memory-status     hit ratio + top recalls + audit tail
     /rulake-memory:memory-promote    pre-warm a collection
     /rulake-memory:memory-tune       recommend Consistency settings per collection
                                      based on observed access patterns
     /rulake-memory:memory-replay     re-issue last N queries vs current state;
                                      surface witness-drift report
     /rulake-memory:memory-compact    pin warm-cache + audit summary into a
                                      long-term memory bundle (survives restarts)

   Plus an auto-invokable skill (skills/memory/SKILL.md) that turns
   ruLake into the agent's working memory transparently — Claude calls
   memory-recall before answering, memory-remember after deciding.

   Three feedback loops:
     - cache learning   (recall stats → auto-promote frequently-recalled keys)
     - consistency learning (tune analyzes hits + drift → recommends Fresh /
                              Eventual{ttl} / Frozen per collection)
     - drift learning   (replay re-runs past queries → witness diff reports)

2. /rulake-core:rulake-discover — advisory entry-point.

   Reads the marketplace catalog, cross-references the locally-installed
   plugin set, and recommends what to install for a given goal. Two
   modes:

     /rulake-core:rulake-discover                    full catalog summary
                                                     (8 plugins, install status)
     /rulake-core:rulake-discover goal="..."         best-match recommendation
                                                     plus alternatives + complementary

   Advisory only — never installs anything, just outputs the install
   command the agent can hand to the user.

3. rulake-stack v2.3.0-alpha.4 — bumped to bundle:
     +memory-recall  +memory-remember  +rulake-discover  (3 new commands)
     description updated to mention rulake-memory in the bundle list.
   The version bump invalidates the install cache so existing operators
   pick up the new content on /plugin update.

4. rulake-core v2.3.0-alpha.2 — bumped to ship the new /rulake-discover
   command. Same cache-invalidation reason.

Marketplace count: 7 -> 8 plugins (one killer-path + seven composable).

Validated with `claude --plugin-dir`: all 11 new commands+skill
resolved (8 memory commands + memory skill + rulake-discover +
existing rulake-query).

  "Witness-anchored every result"
    -> "Cryptographic receipt on every answer"
       + "Each result is fingerprinted; tampered or stale data is
          rejected, not served"
  "<60-second first query"
    -> "Working in under a minute"
       + "Type four lines, get a real query result with a receipt back"

       + "Talks to ... out of the box"

    -> "18 ready-to-use tools"
       + "8 retrieval (search / verify / explain / refresh) + 5
          genomic + 5 quantum"

    -> "A decision trace with every answer"
       + plain-English description of what's in it

    -> "Verify an answer without the network"

    -> "Optional speed-ups for big workloads"

     /rulake-stack:rulake-query  ← works after rulake-stack install
     /rulake-stack:rulake-verify
     /rulake-stack:rulake-bundle-info

   Plus a one-line note that operators wanting the per-source
   namespace (/rulake-core:* etc.) need to install those plugins
   individually.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

readme: split into minimal README + USERGUIDE.md

The previous README was 1,230 lines — informative but heavy for the
"first 30 seconds on the repo" surface. Splitting it follows the
pattern docs/userguide/ already uses: short top-of-funnel,
bottom-of-funnel for the deep stuff.

                  - "where to next" links to USERGUIDE / userguide / gists

                  - architecture diagram
                  - ruLake-vs-typical-vector-DBs (collapsed)
                  - 35-capability matrix (collapsed)
                  - why ruLake exists, performance, examples,
                    status, comparison, ecosystem
                  - everything else from the old README, unchanged

Three paths to depth from README:
  USERGUIDE.md       → general reference

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

readme: correct Claude Code install instructions to verified flow

After end-to-end testing the marketplace install on a real Claude
Code session, two things differ from the original quick-start:

1. Slash commands resolve via the <plugin>:<command> namespace,
   not the bare /rulake-query form. So:
     /rulake-core:rulake-query "..."
     /rulake-witness:rulake-verify path/to/bundle.json
     /rulake-witness:rulake-bundle-info path/to/bundle.json

   Type /rul in Claude Code to autocomplete.

2. The /reload-plugins step is mandatory (Claude Code's install
   message says so) — the original quick-start had this but the
   comment now matches what Claude Code shows.

Also added the verify + bundle-info commands to the example block
since they're part of the rulake-stack install, and reframed the
explanation to call out the decision_trace contract from ADR-009
(the cost-in-relative-units block that comes back on every query).

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

plugins: fix command frontmatter + skill subdirectory layout

After /reload-plugins reported "0 skills" and `/rulake-query`
returned "No commands match", investigated the official
claude-plugins-official format and found two structural issues:

1. Commands: frontmatter must contain ONLY `description` (and
   optionally `argument-hint`, `allowed-tools`). Command name is
   derived from the filename. My commands had `name: rulake-query`
   which Claude Code's loader probably rejected.

   Stripped `name:` from all 8 command files:
     plugins/rulake-core/commands/rulake-query.md
     plugins/rulake-kernels/commands/rulake-kernel-{bench,status}.md
     plugins/rulake-stack/commands/rulake-{query,verify,bundle-info}.md
     plugins/rulake-witness/commands/rulake-{verify,bundle-info}.md

2. Skills: must live in a subdirectory as `<skill-name>/SKILL.md`,
   not as a flat `<skill-name>.md` in skills/. Confirmed against
   plugins/session-report/skills/session-report/SKILL.md in the
   official directory.

   Moved:  plugins/rulake-core/skills/rulake-query.md
   To:     plugins/rulake-core/skills/rulake-query/SKILL.md

After this push:
  /plugin marketplace update rulake-marketplace
  /plugin install rulake-stack@rulake-marketplace  (or already installed)
  /reload-plugins
should report "1 skill" (the rulake-query skill in rulake-core)
and `/rulake-query` should be a recognized slash command from
rulake-stack.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

plugins: bundle commands into rulake-stack + commands/ vs skills/ fix

The marketplace + install both worked, but `/rulake-query` came back
as "Unknown command" because of two structural issues:

1. /rulake-query was in skills/ (Claude-auto-invokable), not
   commands/ (literal slash). Promoted to a command in rulake-core
   and kept in skills for the auto-invoke path too.

2. rulake-stack only bundled MCP wires (.mcp.json), not the
   commands from rulake-core / rulake-witness. So installing
   rulake-stack alone got the user a working MCP wire but no
   slash commands. Bundled rulake-query + rulake-verify +
   rulake-bundle-info into plugins/rulake-stack/commands/ so a
   single install delivers the full killer-path surface.

3. README quick-start gains the /reload-plugins step (Claude Code's
   install message says "Run /reload-plugins to apply" but the
   README didn't mention it). Now the four-line flow is:
     /plugin marketplace add ruvnet/RuLake
     /plugin install rulake-stack@rulake-marketplace
     /reload-plugins
     /rulake-query "..."

After this push, /plugin install rulake-stack gets the user three
slash commands (/rulake-query, /rulake-verify, /rulake-bundle-info)
plus three live MCP wires in one install.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

plugins+phase2: decision_trace shape + author rUv/cognitum.one + README install

Three things in one push so the marketplace lands cleanly on main:

1. Phase 2 of ADR-009 — the decision_trace block on rulake_query.
   The shape ADR-009 promised. New types in planner.rs:
     DecisionTrace, FreshnessTrace, CacheTrace, KernelTrace,
     WitnessTrace, CostTrace, LatencyTrace, TracedQueryResponse.
   DecisionTrace::derive() builds it from the existing Provenance +
   Decision + elapsed_ms — no QueryResponse construction site
   changes. server.rs's rulake_query handler wraps the response in
   TracedQueryResponse before returning, so the wire schema gains
   `decision_trace` alongside the existing data/provenance/decision/
   trust_level fields. Backwards compatible.

   v0.1 ships the SHAPE with conservative placeholders:
     - chosen_path: "deterministic-retrieval-path-v0.1"
     - cost: relative-units (compute_kernel=0.0,
                             backend_fetch=N_backends_used,
                             cache_hit_discount=-1.0 if hit)
     - cache.hit_ratio_session: null (planner doesn't track yet)
     - latency.{cache,fanout,witness}_ms: null (per-step
       instrumentation lands in v2.4)
     - kernel: cpu-naive default (no kernel registry exposed yet)

   Tests: 40/40 mcp-server tests pass.

2. Marketplace authorship: owner.name and per-plugin author.name are
   now `rUv`; owner.email and per-plugin author.email are
   `hello@cognitum.one`. Applied across .claude-plugin/marketplace.json
   and the six plugins/*/.claude-plugin/plugin.json files.

3. README quick-start gets a Claude Code install block:
     /plugin marketplace add ruvnet/RuLake
     /plugin install rulake-stack@rulake-marketplace
     /rulake-query "..."

   Sits next to the four cargo/pip/npm install paths so users
   landing on the README see all five surfaces.

Once this hits main, /plugin marketplace add ruvnet/RuLake resolves
the catalog directly off the default branch — users can install
without any further action from us.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

plugins: ship Phase 1 of ADR-009 marketplace (6 plugins + CI)

Implements Phase 1 of the marketplace per ADR-009:

  .claude-plugin/marketplace.json                        — the catalog
  plugins/rulake-stack/                                  — killer-path
  plugins/rulake-core/                                   — 8 rulake_* tools
  plugins/rulake-substrates/                             — 4 adapters + 2 MCPs
  plugins/rulake-kernels/                                — ADR-157 kernels
  plugins/rulake-witness/                                — verify CLI
  plugins/rulake-loop-vector/                            — /loop workers
  .github/workflows/validate-marketplace.yml             — CI gate

Each plugin ships:
  .claude-plugin/plugin.json   (name + version + description + author)
  .mcp.json                    (HTTP wire to live demo, where applicable)
  README.md                    (what's installed + see-also links)
  commands/skills/             (slash-command surfaces)

Plugin layout:
  rulake-stack    — bundles core + substrates + witness; 3 MCP wires
                    (rulake-mcp.ruv.io, rvdna-mcp.ruv.io, ruqu-mcp.ruv.io)
  rulake-core     — single rulake wire; /rulake-query skill
  rulake-substrates — rvdna + ruqu wires
  rulake-kernels  — no MCP (in-process); /rulake-kernel-status,
                    /rulake-kernel-bench commands
  rulake-witness  — no MCP (local verify); /rulake-verify,
                    /rulake-bundle-info commands
  rulake-loop-vector — skill-only; composes core+substrates

Local validation passed:
  ✓ marketplace.json parses
  ✓ all 6 plugin manifests parse with required fields
  ✓ all 3 .mcp.json wires parse
  ✓ marketplace name fields match plugin.json names

CI workflow validate-marketplace.yml runs on .claude-plugin/, plugins/,
or workflow change. Five gates:
  1. marketplace.json parses
  2. every source: path exists
  3. every plugin has plugin.json with required fields
  4. plugin name in manifest matches marketplace entry
  5. all .mcp.json files parse

After this commit:
  /plugin marketplace add ruvnet/RuLake
  /plugin install rulake-stack@rulake-marketplace
  /rulake-query "..."

resolves end-to-end against the live Cloud Run demo.

Phase 2 (decision_trace response shape) and Phase 3 (Console
/marketplace route) follow as separate commits.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

adr-009 (revised): killer-path plugin + cost-aware response + named flow

Strategic feedback on the first revision pushed back on three things:

1. Five plugins is a shopping decision, not an install. Add a
   sixth — `rulake-stack` — that's the 90% case (core + substrates
   + witness, kernels off by default), marketed as the default
   install. The composable plugins stay for operators who already
   know they want a subset.

2. retrieval is opaque if the dispatch policy isn't surfaced.
   Lock a `decision_trace` block on every `rulake_query` response:
   chosen_path, intent, freshness budget+actual, cache hit/ratio,
   substrates_used, kernel id+deterministic flag, witness
   expected/computed/match, cost (compute_kernel + backend_fetch
   + cache_hit_discount in relative-units), latency breakdown,
   refusals[]. This is "economic routing telemetry" — not USD
   billing, but the numbers a calling agent uses to negotiate.

3. The default flow needs a name. Adopt "deterministic retrieval
   path":
     intent → cache check → substrate fanout → mincut prune
            → witness verify → return
   Each step maps to an existing crate file. The mincut-prune step
   is v2.4 roadmap (crates/core/src/select.rs doesn't exist yet),
   so v0.1 ships as `deterministic-retrieval-path-v0.1` with no
   prune; the upgrade is visible in decision_trace.chosen_path.

Also added:

- One-line positioning at the top: "ruLake turns retrieval into a
  first-class, installable primitive for agents."
- The "first-query-in-60-seconds" success metric for the killer
  path: marketplace add → install rulake-stack → /rulake-query
  a real question → get result + decision_trace.
- "Failure modes" table with 4 entries (plugin fatigue, trust gap,
  demo misuse, latency variance) + the structural mitigation each
  one already has in this design.
- "Design questions resolved" section answering both questions
  the first revision left open:
    Q: invisible infrastructure or developer-facing product?
    A: both, at different layers — invisible at the agent layer
       (one MCP tool), developer-facing at the operator layer
       (Console, CLI, plugins, audit ledger).
    Q: should rulake_query expose policy inputs?
    A: expose them. Hiding policy is the failure mode of every
       opaque router that came before. Inputs stay simple (intent
       / risk / freshness / target); outputs go rich
       (decision_trace is part of the contract, not a debug field).

The contrastive-retrieval surface (mincut prune as "find boundary-
defining data" rather than "find similar") is documented as ruLake's
unique unlock that no other retrieval layer ships cleanly today.

Open questions down from 5 to 4 (plugin signing folded into trust
posture).

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

adr-009: ruLake as Claude Code plugin marketplace + Pages storefront

Locks the packaging shape for a ruLake-flavored marketplace,
patterned on the ruvnet/ruflo proposal but retargeted to ruLake's
witness-anchored vector-federation capabilities (the 8 rulake_*
tools, the 4 backend adapters, the 2 ADR-157 kernels, the witness
verification CLI, the /loop-aware vector workers).

Five plugins, not one mega-plugin and not 10+ per-crate plugins:

  rulake-core         The 8 MCP tools + rulake_query decision layer.
                      Default wire: https://rulake-mcp.ruv.io/ (the
                      live Cloud Run demo) or local stdio.
  rulake-substrates   gcs / ipfs / rvdna / ruqu adapters + the two
                      companion MCP servers (rvdna-mcp, ruqu-mcp).
  rulake-kernels      ADR-157 accelerator plane (AVX-512 + wgpu).
                      Off-by-default; operator opt-in.
  rulake-witness      SHAKE-256(32) bundle verification + viewer CLI.
  rulake-loop-vector  /loop-aware workers — incremental indexing,
                      refresh-from-bundle, witness-mismatch
                      refuse-and-replan.

Repo layout is additive: .claude-plugin/marketplace.json + a
plugins/ directory; crates/, sdk/, ui/, docs/ unchanged. Plugins
reference existing binaries (no code duplication).

GitHub Pages stays discovery-only — the existing Console at
ruvnet.github.io/RuLake gains a /marketplace route with per-plugin
landing tiles + install-command copy widgets. The actual install is
Claude Code's /plugin install reading marketplace.json on GitHub.

Trust posture: pin versions in marketplace.json, default plugins
to --capabilities read (not read,publish,admin), document the
public-demo --insecure-allow-no-auth caveat, tag releases, and
forward-commit to gh plugin sign when Anthropic ships it.

CI gain: one new workflow (validate-marketplace.yml) that checks
JSON parse, source: paths exist, version fields match parent crate
versions, and storefront install commands resolve via gh api.

5 alternatives walked + rejected (single mega-plugin, one-per-crate,
separate repo, no per-plugin folders, Pages-as-installer). 5 open
questions tracked (kernel-host requirements, loop-vector skill-only
shape, claude-plugins-official submission timing, plugin signing,
docs/userguide overlap).

This is packaging + discovery; no new core code committed by this
ADR. The implementation lands when the plugins/ directory + the
marketplace.json + the storefront route get scaffolded — out of
scope for this decision.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

kernel-wgpu: act on R-WGPU-1 — per-call dispatch byte cap

The MEDIUM finding from docs/research/security/kernels-v2.md: both
WgpuKernel dispatch helpers allocate `dim * n * elem_bytes` on the
host before any wgpu-side limit check, so a malicious n*dim shape
could OOM the calling thread.

Fix:
- Add `pub const MAX_DISPATCH_BYTES: u64 = 1 << 30` (1 GiB)
- Cap check at the entry of `l2_distance_one` and `rabitq_popcount`
  using `saturating_mul` to prevent overflow bypass
- Over-cap requests return empty top-K + single eprintln! warning
- Three unit tests covering both paths + sanity under-cap test

Tests: 3/3 pass on host with NVIDIA via Vulkan adapter.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

security: focused review of ADR-157 kernels (avx-512 + wgpu)

Closes the gap from the previous two security passes
(`v0.0-substrates.md` covered rvdna+ruqu backends;
`shipping-substrates-v2.md` covered gcs+ipfs+mcp-server).
The two ADR-157 kernels shipped after both passes and hadn't been
audited.

Reviewed:
  crates/kernel-avx512/src/lib.rs  (406 lines, 9 unsafe blocks under
                                    runtime CPUID + #[target_feature])
  crates/kernel-wgpu/src/lib.rs    (649 lines, #![forbid(unsafe_code)])
  crates/kernel-wgpu/shaders/{l2,popcount}.wgsl  (74 lines combined)

Findings (1 MEDIUM, 4 LOW, 2 INFO):

  R-AVX-1   LOW    Popcount accumulator can theoretically truncate
                   above 64M dim (structurally unreachable on real
                   RaBitQ workloads; recommend debug_assert)
  R-AVX-2   INFO   _mm512_mul_ps_local shim widens unsafe surface
                   by one helper (cosmetic)
  R-WGPU-1  MEDIUM No per-call resource cap on host-side or GPU-side
                   buffer allocation. A malicious n*dim could OOM
                   the host. Recommend max_dispatch_bytes in caps.
  R-WGPU-2  LOW    No timeout on device.poll(Maintain::Wait) — a
                   driver hang permanently consumes a worker slot.
  R-WGPU-3  LOW    Two stacked expect() panics on driver edge cases.
                   GPU crash takes down a worker thread.
  R-WGPU-4  LOW    Silent little-endian assumption in u64->2*u32
                   pack. Should be a compile_error! on big-endian.
  R-WGPU-5  INFO   Per-call buffer allocation (perf note interacting
                   with the v1.x device-side cache roadmap)

No memory-safety, witness-bypass, or cross-query-leakage findings.
Both kernels are off-by-default; operators register them explicitly
per ADR-157, so none of these findings are exploitable on the
current shipping configuration.

The MEDIUM finding (R-WGPU-1) becomes a real concern when
WgpuKernel is wired into mcp-server's bounded rayon pool — that's
where an n*dim*4 cap converts an OOM into a structured refusal.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

bench: capture three-way kernel L2 sweep (CpuNaive / AVX-512 / wgpu)

ADR-157 ships two kernels off-by-default, with promotion past
experimental gated on "2x p95 lower OR 30% cost lower at identical
recall@10" on a fixed reference grid. This is the bench session that
runs all three shipped kernels against the same headline workload
(D=384, n=16384, top_k=10) and lays the numbers next to each other.

Headline results on AMD Ryzen 9 9950X + NVIDIA via Vulkan:

  CpuNaive (core)              2.8065 ms    1.00x  (baseline)
  AVX-512                      2.7082 ms    0.971x (2.87% faster)
  wgpu (RTX 5080 via Vulkan)   7.8270 ms    2.756x (slower)

Verdict on this grid: neither kernel passes the ADR-157 acceptance
gate. Both stay off-by-default, exactly as the trait + dispatch
design intended. The doc explains why:

- AVX-512 wins only ~2.87% because the host-side top-k sort dominates
  the budget at this grid (the inner loop is already cheap).
- wgpu loses 2.76x because host->device transfer dominates the work,
  and the current implementation re-uploads the index on every scan
  (no generation-keyed device-side cache yet).

Both kernels' wins should materialize at the ADR-157 reference grid
(D=768 / n=1M / batch=64+). The doc closes with the two specific
bench-file changes to make next: extend the grid, add a warm-cache
path so the wgpu device-side index cache (planned for v1.x) can be
measured against the upload-per-scan baseline.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

commit.message:
feat(pointcloud): fix upside-down face, densify mesh, add Foundation aesthetic

Three fixes in one pass to address visitor feedback:

1. Face was rendering upside down — MediaPipe's lm.y is image-down (0=top
   of frame, 1=bottom) and the existing updateSplats() already does a
   y-negate to convert to Three.js Y-up. Pre-flipping in lmToCenter was a
   double flip. Use lm.y directly so the renderer's single flip lands the
   head at the top of the screen.

2. Density and fidelity — interpolate 6 splats per FACEMESH_TESSELATION
   edge (~1300 edges → ~8000 face splats vs 478 vertex-only). Amplify
   lm.z mapping (×8 vs ×4) so eye sockets, nose, and chin show real 3D
   depth. Smaller splat scale (0.006 surface, 0.010 vertices) for finer
   point appearance.

3. Foundation-inspired aesthetic — the demo now renders the subject
   (face mesh OR procedural fallback) inside a Hari Seldon time-vault:

   * Holographic surveyor grid in amber, breathing brightness pattern.
   * Slow-rotating two-arm galactic spiral receding behind the subject
     (~640 stars, warm core to cool edges, Trantor-evocation).
   * 800-star deterministic distant starfield on a spherical shell
     (fixed LCG seed so visitors don't see noise flicker).
   * 60-particle holographic halo orbiting the subject plane.

   Shared pushFoundationContext() drives both face-mesh and synthetic
   paths. Synthetic procedural figure densified 4x (240 vs 60 points)
   and re-oriented (head→top, feet→bottom) so the y-down convention is
   internally consistent.

Camera pulled back to (0, 0.2, -3.5) to frame the galactic context.
Poll cadence 4 Hz → 10 Hz so the spiral animates smoothly. Info panel
gets a Seldon quote and "Seldon Vault" branding. CTA copy reframed to
"Project Subject — render your face into the Vault".

ADR-094 already documents the dual-transport intent; the aesthetic
choices here are content, not architecture, so no ADR update needed.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

feat(pointcloud): use MediaPipe Face Mesh for the live demo (ADR-094)

The previous synthetic procedural demo did not represent what the local
fusion pipeline produces — a real depth-backprojected point cloud of
the user's face and surroundings. This commit ports the closest browser
equivalent: MediaPipe Face Mesh runs in-browser at ~30 fps and emits
478 3D landmarks per frame. Each visitor now sees the outline of their
own face rendered as a point cloud, with a small floor + back wall for
spatial context.

- Adds MediaPipe Face Mesh + Camera Utils via jsdelivr CDN.
- Adds an "▶ Enable camera" CTA so getUserMedia is gated on a user
  gesture (required by some browsers and good UX regardless).
- New face-mesh frame generator uses the same splat shape as the live
  /api/splats payload, so a single render path drives both modes.
- Mirrors x to match selfie convention; maps lm.z (relative depth) to
  the world-coord range used by the live pipeline.
- Falls back automatically to the procedural floor + walls + figure
  when the camera is denied, dismissed, or unavailable.
- Badge surfaces the new state: '● DEMO Your Face (MediaPipe)'.
- Bumps poll cadence to 4 Hz so face mesh updates feel live.
- ADR-094 updated to reflect the new default behavior.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

docs(readme): retarget Live 3D Point Cloud link to hosted demo

Now that ADR-094 is deployed, point the README's demo link at
https://ruvnet.github.io/RuView/pointcloud/ instead of the
docs/readme-details.md anchor. Matches the pattern of the sibling
Observatory and Pose Fusion demo links.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

feat(pointcloud): GitHub Pages demo with optional live backend (ADR-094) (#495)

Publishes the live 3D point cloud viewer to gh-pages/pointcloud/ so it
can be linked from the README alongside the Observatory and Dual-Modal
Pose Fusion demos. The viewer auto-selects its transport from URL
parameters:

- default / ?backend=auto — try /api/splats, fall back to synthetic demo
- ?backend=demo — synthetic in-browser only, no network
- ?backend=<url> — fetch from a CORS-permitting host running
  ruview-pointcloud serve
- ?live=1 — strict mode, show offline panel instead of demo fallback

The synthetic frame matches the live API JSON shape (splats, count,
frame, live, pipeline.{skeleton,vitals}) so a single render path drives
both modes. New workflow uses keep_files: true to preserve the existing
observatory/, pose-fusion/, and nvsim/ deployments on gh-pages.

See docs/adr/ADR-094-pointcloud-github-pages-deployment.md for the full
decision record and 6 acceptance gates.

- Move Latest Additions, Key Features, and everything from Installation
  through Changelog (1855 lines) into docs/readme-details.md.
- Keep README focused on overview, capability table, How It Works,
  Use Cases, Documentation, License, and Support.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

Add 6 plugins, ADR, marketplace updates, and protocol test harness

New plugins:
- agentics-tutorials: getting-started/build-agent/mcp-integration skills, /tutorial, agentics-tutor agent
- agentics-onboarding: 4-step new-member flow, /onboard, onboarding-guide agent
- agentics-glossary: /glossary <term> with embedded definitions
- agentics-events: MCP server for Luma API (list/search/get_event), with fallback when LUMA_API_KEY unset
- agentics-blog: MCP server for blog content (RSS-first, sitemap fallback)
- agentics-projects: MCP server for agenticsorg GitHub repos + agentics.org/partners

Marketplace:
- Register 7 plugins in .claude-plugin/marketplace.json
- Refresh root README with plugin table, composition diagram, contribution rules
- Add ADR-0001 documenting the monorepo marketplace structure decision
- Add test-all-mcp.js: drives each MCP server through initialize/tools/list/tools/call

All 4 MCP servers pass the protocol test against live agentics.org / Luma / GitHub.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

Restructure as Claude Code marketplace

Adds .claude-plugin/marketplace.json at repo root so the repo can be added
via `/plugin marketplace add agenticsorg/claude-plugin`. The agentics-org
plugin is moved under plugins/agentics-org/.

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

Initial commit: agentics.org Claude Code plugin

MCP server exposing the public agentics.org website to Claude Code:
- Tools: fetch_page, search_content, list_pages
- Resources: agentics://home, /about, /community, /events, /blog, /contact, /membership, /projects
- Prompts: summarize-section, answer-from-site
- Skill, slash command (/agentics), and Sonnet research agent

Co-Authored-By: claude-flow <ruv@ruv.net>

feat(seed): cognitum-seed plugin — 114 MCP tools + 8 slash commands (#1)

Adds the cognitum-seed plugin alongside the existing cognitum-mcp plugin.
Targets a local Cognitum Seed appliance at 169.254.42.1 (USB-C link-local
or LAN IP via WiFi).

Components
- .mcp.json registers the device's local MCP server. Defaults to
  http://169.254.42.1/mcp (USB gadget mode, no auth). Override to
  https://<wifi-ip>:8443/mcp + COGNITUM_SEED_TOKEN bearer for WiFi mode.
- 8 slash commands: /seed-pair, /seed-status, /seed-wifi, /seed-ota,
  /seed-thermal, /seed-store, /seed-snapshot, /seed-cluster.
- seed-installer agent walks first-time users through pair → identity →
  WiFi → firmware → token-handoff with explicit confirmations on
  destructive steps.
- seed-troubleshoot skill gives Claude a playbook for common failure
  modes (pairing timeout, 403s, OTA stalls, missing MCP tools, witness
  chain gaps).

Endpoints wrapped (subset): /pair, /status, /identity, /wifi/*,
/firmware/*, /upgrade/*, /thermal/*, /store/*, /cognitive/*, /witness/*,
/peers, /cluster/health.

Lives in cognitum-seed/ subdirectory so the cognitum-mcp plugin at the
repo root keeps working unchanged. Marketplace.json on cognitum.one
needs to be updated to list both plugins (separate commit on the
newport repo).

Co-authored-by: Cognitum <noreply@cognitum.one>

commit.message:
fix(federation): publish @claude-flow/plugin-agent-federation@1.0.0-alpha.3 with ruflo-federation bin (#1685)

Same class of bug as iot-cognitum: source existed (325 tests passing) but
unpublished, and ruflo-federation marketplace plugin's skills called
`npx @claude-flow/cli@latest federation <subcmd>` — federation isn't a
CLI subcommand. End result: every federation skill failed.

Plugin source (v3/@claude-flow/plugin-agent-federation):
- New src/bin.ts: standalone CLI shim that bootstraps AgentFederationPlugin
  in-memory, parses argv, resolves multi-word commands (federation peers
  add, federation peers remove, etc.), aliases short flags, dispatches to
  CLICommandDefinition handlers. Loads .env for FEDERATION_NODE_NAME,
  FEDERATION_BIND_HOST, FEDERATION_BIND_PORT, FEDERATION_TRUST_LEVEL.
- package.json: add bin {ruflo-federation: dist/bin.js}, files whitelist,
  homepage. Move workspace deps to devDependencies (all imports are
  `import type`, erased at compile — zero runtime workspace dep).
- New README.md with subcommand reference and .env config.

Marketplace plugin (plugins/ruflo-federation):
- Bulk-rewrote 5 files (3 skills + commands/federation.md +
  agents/federation-coordinator.md): now call
  `npx -y -p @claude-flow/plugin-agent-federation@latest ruflo-federation <subcmd>`

Other minor fix:
- plugins/ruflo-swarm/README.md: pin `npx @claude-flow/cli swarm` →
  `npx @claude-flow/cli@latest swarm` for consistency with the other 31 plugins.

Audit result post-fix: 32/32 plugins use working invocation patterns.
- 12 verbs against @claude-flow/cli@latest (all exist: memory, hooks,
  security, neural, swarm, doctor, init, embeddings, status, daemon,
  config, plus session)
- @claude-flow/plugin-iot-cognitum (alpha.5 published)
- @claude-flow/plugin-agent-federation (alpha.3 published — this PR)
- neural-trader (2.7.1 on npm)
- ruvector (0.2.25 on npm)

Smoke test: `npx -y -p @claude-flow/plugin-agent-federation@latest
ruflo-federation status` returns Node ID + session count + healthy flag.

Caveat: the 11 plugins that depend on mcp__claude-flow__* MCP tools
(adr, aidefence, autopilot, browser, daa, goals, plugin-creator, ruvllm,
sparc, wasm, workflows) require the MCP server to be connected — not
audited here.

Co-authored-by: Reuven <cohen@ruv-mac-mini.local>

commit.message:
feat(iot): COGNITUM_SEED_TOKEN bearer auth via .env (1.0.0-alpha.5) (#1684)

The bin shim now loads .env from CWD (or any parent) without overwriting
existing env vars, then reads COGNITUM_SEED_TOKEN. When present:
  - Default endpoint switches from http://169.254.42.1 (link-local, no auth)
    to https://169.254.42.1:8443 (LAN/HTTPS, bearer)
  - The token is passed as the pairing/bearer token on `register`
  - All subsequent SDK calls use the bearer-authenticated session

Verified end-to-end against a live Seed: bearer-authed register, status,
mesh, witness, query all work over HTTPS:8443. Self-signed dev cert is
tolerated via NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0 in the smoke harness;
plugin/SDK tolerance via tlsInsecure config (default true).

Smoke results with bearer: 22/25 pass. The 3 remaining failures are
real Seed-side state (rate-limited pair window from prior runs, SDK
trust-score circuit breaker after repeated unauthorized attempts in
the same in-process run, and store/ingest correctly classified as
needing a write-scoped token vs. our read-scoped one).

Plugin source (src/bin.ts):
- New loadDotenv() walks up from CWD looking for .env, parses KEY=VALUE
  lines (handles quoted values, comments, blanks), sets process.env
  without overwriting.
- SEED_DEFAULT_ENDPOINT = COGNITUM_SEED_ENDPOINT ?? (token ? lan : link-local)
- register handler auto-injects --token from env if not explicitly passed
- --help banner shows token status and endpoint mode

Smoke harness:
- Loads same .env, picks endpoint+token automatically
- pair-window opener now checks status first (avoids HTTP 429 on already-open),
  passes bearer when available
- ingest failure now classified as expected-on-read-scope-tokens

Plugin README documents .env setup, COGNITUM_SEED_TOKEN, optional
COGNITUM_SEED_ENDPOINT/IOT_FLEET_ID/IOT_ZONE_ID/IOT_TLS_INSECURE overrides,
and the read-vs-write token scope distinction.

Bump to 1.0.0-alpha.5; published to npm (alpha + latest).

Co-authored-by: Reuven <cohen@ruv-mac-mini.local>

commit.message:
feat(iot): full-coverage smoke + iot query/ingest fixes; link to cognitum.one (1.0.0-alpha.4) (#1683)

Drove the live-Seed smoke harness from 11/24 to 24/25 covered, then 21/25
passing on link-local HTTP (the remaining 4 are real Seed security
boundaries, not plugin bugs).

Plugin source fixes (cli-commands.ts):
- iot query: handler now accepts --vector "[..]" JSON arg and parses to
  number[] (was: passed empty array; result was always nearest-neighbour
  to origin)
- iot ingest: handler now accepts --vector "[..]" --metadata "{..}" OR a
  JSON array on stdin (was: stub that always passed []; the SDK rejected
  empty arrays). Uses the SDK's expected {values, metadata} shape.
- New parseVector helper validates JSON array of numbers.

Smoke harness (__tests__/integration/full-plugin-smoke.mjs):
- Rewrote to 25 commands across 7 tiers (init/lifecycle, read-only,
  vector ops, pair round-trip, fleet ops, firmware reads, deregister).
- Pair flow opens pair window via direct API call first (the plugin
  doesn't expose pair-window control yet — known gap).
- Bearer-required failures (unpair, ingest write) classified as expected
  on link-local; firmware deploy/advance/rollback explicitly skipped
  (OTA-can-brick).
- Pass-through dArgs() helper handles cli-commands.ts arg-style
  inconsistency (some handlers read args._[0], others args['device-id']).

Cognitum.one mention (per user request):
- v3/@claude-flow/plugin-iot-cognitum/package.json: description now
  includes "Get a Seed at https://cognitum.one"; added homepage field.
- New README.md for the npm package — full command reference with
  cognitum.one link prominent at the top.
- plugins/ruflo-iot-cognitum/README.md: "Hardware" section added with
  cognitum.one link and link-local + LAN endpoint reference.

Bump to 1.0.0-alpha.4. Published to npm (alpha + latest).

Known gaps for follow-up (not blockers, real Seed limits):
- ingest/unpair require bearer auth via https://...:8443 — plugin doesn't
  support the bearer transport yet
- pair window opening isn't exposed as a CLI command
- iot pair triggers Seed rate-limiter (HTTP 429) when called repeatedly
- SDK's witness.chain() returns 0 length while raw API shows 49,138
  entries — different endpoint shape

Co-authored-by: Reuven <cohen@ruv-mac-mini.local>

commit.message:
fix(iot): register client under both endpoint and device-id during registration (#1682)

The IoTCoordinator.registerDevice() flow was broken: it stored the SeedClient
under the endpoint key only, but DeviceLifecycleService.registerDevice() makes
two SDK calls — getStatus(endpoint) which works, then getIdentity(device_id)
which throws "Device <id> not registered with coordinator" because the client
isn't reachable under the device-id key yet. End result: register always
failed even when the Seed responded correctly.

Fix: pre-fetch the device-id via client.status() in the coordinator, then
register the client under both keys (endpoint and device-id) before invoking
the lifecycle service. After lifecycle returns, drop the endpoint key and
keep the device-id mapping.

Verified end-to-end against a live Cognitum Seed device (firmware 0.8.1):

  $ npx -y -p @claude-flow/plugin-iot-cognitum@latest cognitum-iot register
  [info] No --endpoint supplied; defaulting to http://169.254.42.1
  Device registered: ecaf97dd-fc90-4b0e-b0e7-e9f896b9fbb6
    Endpoint:  http://169.254.42.1
    Trust:     registered
    Firmware:  0.8.1

Full-suite live smoke (register → status → mesh → witness → witness verify
→ anomalies → baseline → fleet create/list) all pass. Trust score 0.800
(certified). 239 unit tests still pass.

Bumps to 1.0.0-alpha.3, published to npm (alpha + latest).
Adds __tests__/integration/full-plugin-smoke.mjs as the chained-command
integration harness (the published bin is one-shot; this script bootstraps
the plugin once and exercises device-aware commands in a single process).

Co-authored-by: Reuven <cohen@ruv-mac-mini.local>

commit.message:
feat(iot): ship @claude-flow/plugin-iot-cognitum@1.0.0-alpha.2 with cognitum-iot bin (#1681)

ADR-079 was implemented (239 tests passing across 19 test files) but never
shipped as an installable npm package — the marketplace plugin's skills
referenced `npx @claude-flow/cli@latest iot ...` which the CLI never exposed.

Plugin changes (v3/@claude-flow/plugin-iot-cognitum):
- Add src/bin.ts: standalone CLI shim that bootstraps the plugin in-memory,
  parses argv, resolves multi-word commands (iot fleet add, iot firmware
  deploy, etc.), aliases short flags, and dispatches to CLICommandDefinition
  handlers. Defaults `iot register` endpoint to http://169.254.42.1 (the
  Cognitum Seed link-local USB Ethernet address).
- package.json: add `bin: {cognitum-iot: dist/bin.js}`, declare files
  whitelist, bump to 1.0.0-alpha.2, move @claude-flow/shared to
  devDependencies (all imports are `import type`, erased at compile)

Marketplace plugin changes (plugins/ruflo-iot-cognitum):
- Replace 10 files' `npx @claude-flow/cli@latest iot ...` invocations with
  `npx -y -p @claude-flow/plugin-iot-cognitum@latest cognitum-iot ...`
- iot register endpoint argument now optional with documented default

Published @claude-flow/plugin-iot-cognitum@1.0.0-alpha.2 to npm
(tags: alpha + latest). Smoke-tested init / list / fleet list / register
default-endpoint via npx end-to-end.

Co-authored-by: Reuven <cohen@ruv-mac-mini.local>

commit.message:
fix: 4 high-priority bugs — browser ENOENT, memory path, stdio crash, settings (#1604, #1605, #1606, #1616)

#1605: Guard browser_* MCP tool registration behind agent-browser
availability check. Tools are no longer advertised when the binary
is missing.

#1604: Align memory-tools.ts MEMORY_DIR with memory-initializer.ts
(.swarm/memory.db). Legacy migration now correctly reads from the
old .claude-flow/memory/ path.

#1606: Wrap ensureInitialized() in try/catch to prevent unhandled
exceptions from killing the stdio transport. Fix cli.js outer catch
to return -32603 (internal error) instead of -32700 (parse error)
for handleMessage failures.

#1616: Rename .claude/settings copy.json → settings.json.bak so
VSCode Copilot no longer treats it as an active settings file.

Co-authored-by: Reuven <cohen@ruv-mac-mini.local>
